topics.title: Adaptive Control - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Control - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Control** is a class of advanced control techniques in which a controller automatically adjusts its parameters in real-time to compensate for variations in the dynamics of the controlled process and/or disturbances, in order to maintain a desired level of performance when the process parameters are unknown, time-varying, or both.

In the formal KTU 2024 scheme sense, an **adaptive control system** is one that incorporates an online **parameter estimation block** and a **control law synthesis block** that work in tandem. The system continuously measures plant input–output data, estimates the unknown or drifting parameters $\hat{\theta}(t)$ of a chosen parametric plant model, and uses this estimate to recompute the controller gains. The objective is to keep the closed-loop response stable and close to a reference behavior despite **parametric uncertainty** and **structural disturbance**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Driving Instructor**

Imagine a learner driver practicing on a flat, dry road in Kerala summer. Initially, the instructor must be told "press the brake gently and steer smoothly." After 10 minutes, the learner has built a mental model of the car. Now take that same car to a **monsoon-flooded Munnar ghat road**. The car dynamics have completely changed — the same steering angle produces a different response because of low friction, water on the tyres, and reduced visibility.

A *non-adaptive* controller would still apply the original steering/pedal mapping, fail, and the car skids.

An *adaptive* controller behaves like an **experienced driver who keeps updating his mental model of the car–road combination**: he measures slip, speed, lateral force, and re-tunes his "control law" (muscle memory) in real-time. That is precisely what adaptive control does — it **learns the plant as it operates** and **recomputes its own tuning law** continuously.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Adaptive Control

> [!NOTE]
> The performance of any adaptive scheme in KTU examinations is judged using the following benchmark quantities:
> - **Tracking Error**: $e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$ — deviation from a reference model.
> - **Parameter Error**: $\tilde{\theta}(t) = \theta(t) - \hat{\theta}(t)$ — drift between true and estimated parameters.
> - **Closed-Loop Stability** in the sense of **Lyapunov** or **Input-to-State Stability (ISS)**.
> - **Convergence Rate** — typically exponential in deterministic MRAC and asymptotic in stochastic STR.

### 1.4 When Do We Need Adaptive Control?

| Condition | Description | Adaptive Control Required? |
|---|---|---|
| **Known constant plant** | $\theta$ fixed and known | No — use fixed-gain PID |
| **Slowly time-varying plant** | $\dot{\theta}(t) \neq 0$, small drift | Yes — Gain Scheduling or MRAC |
| **Unknown constant plant** | $\theta$ unknown, time-invariant | Yes — MRAC, STR |
| **Noisy / stochastic plant** | Random disturbances, ARMA structure | Yes — Self-Tuning Regulators |
| **Highly nonlinear plant** | Multiple operating regimes | Sometimes — combined with gain scheduling |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Adaptive Control

Any adaptive system is built from three core building blocks:

1. **Plant (Process) Model** — A parametric model of the system whose parameters must be identified/updated.
2. **Parameter Estimation / Identification Law** — A rule (e.g., gradient descent, least squares, MIT rule) that updates $\hat{\theta}(t)$.
3. **Control Law (Controller Synthesis)** — A rule that uses $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to compute the control action $u(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Marks in the 14-mark question on Adaptive Control — Introduction are usually awarded for correctly identifying and *explaining the role* of these three blocks and their interaction.

### 2.2 Classification of Adaptive Control Schemes

Adaptive control techniques are classified into **four major families**, all of which are examinable:

- **Gain Scheduling** — A non-linear but *non-learning* approach where controller gains are pre-computed as functions of auxiliary measurable variables (operating points). It is technically a "look-up table" of PID gains.
- **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)** — A reference model $y_m(t)$ is specified, and an adaptation law drives the plant output $y(t)$ to track $y_m(t)$. The structure is derived from the **MIT rule** or **Lyapunov theory**.
- **Self-Tuning Regulators (STR)** — An online identifier (e.g., Recursive Least Squares) estimates plant parameters, and a separate synthesis block (e.g., pole placement, minimum-variance) recomputes the controller.
- **Dual Control** — A theoretically optimal but rarely implemented scheme (Feldbaum) where control actions serve both **probing** (learning) and **regulation** simultaneously.

### 2.3 Key Differences: Adaptive vs. Non-Adaptive (Robust) Control

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite 3-mark short-answer question** in KTU 2024 scheme.

| Property | Robust (Non-Adaptive) Control | Adaptive Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Plant knowledge** | Known bounds on uncertainty | Unknown / varying parameters |
| **Controller structure** | Fixed after design | Parameters updated online |
| **Design stage** | Offline, once | Online, continuous |
| **Computation** | Low | High (real-time estimation) |
| **Stability proof** | $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ / $\mu$-synthesis | Lyapunov / Passivity / ISS |
| **Convergence guarantee** | Not required | Yes (under persistent excitation) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. It contains every equation that has appeared or can appear in Module 1 of the KTU 2024 Adaptive Control paper.

| Concept | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **General parametric plant** | $y(t) = \theta^{\top} \phi(t) + e(t)$ | $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ unknown, $\phi$ regressor |
| **Tracking error** | $e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$ | Scalar deviation signal |
| **Parameter error** | $\tilde{\theta}(t) = \theta - \hat{\theta}(t)$ | Drift vector |
| **MIT rule** | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma e \frac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$ | Gradient-descent adaptation |
| **MIT rule (gain form)** | $\dot{\theta}_c = -\gamma \, e \, y$ | Simple case: gain adaptation |
| **Lyapunov adaptation** | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma \, e \, \phi$ | Stable MRAC adaptation law |
| **RLS update** | $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) [y(t) - \phi^{\top}\hat{\theta}(t-1)]$ | Recursive Least Squares |
| **RLS gain** | $K(t) = \frac{P(t-1)\phi(t)}{\lambda + \phi^{\top}(t) P(t-1)\phi(t)}$ | With forgetting factor $\lambda$ |
| **Lyapunov candidate** | $V = \frac{1}{2}\gamma^{-1}\tilde{\theta}^{\top}\tilde{\theta} + \frac{1}{2}e^2$ | Energy function |
| **Persistent excitation** | $\alpha I \leq \int_{t_0}^{t_0+T} \phi \phi^{\top} d\tau \leq \beta I$ | Ensures parameter convergence |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pipe Hazard Avoided:** All absolute value / norm symbols above use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$, never the bare pipe character, so the table renders correctly.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace** — Aircraft flight control (changing mass, altitude, Mach number).
- **Robotics** — Manipulators with unknown payloads and joint friction.
- **Process control** — Chemical reactors with slow catalyst deactivation.
- **Automotive** — Active suspension, adaptive cruise control, EV motor drives.
- **Power systems** — Adaptive excitation control of synchronous generators.
- **Biomedical** — Drug delivery systems with time-varying patient dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The General Adaptive Control Loop (Closed-Form)

We start with a **single-input single-output (SISO)** plant in the form:

$$
y(t) = G_p(s)[u(t)]
$$

Let the **nominal / reference model** be a stable transfer function $W_m(s)$ driven by a bounded reference $r(t)$:

$$
y_m(t) = W_m(s)[r(t)]
$$

The **tracking error** is the difference between the actual plant output and the model output:

$$
e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Define the control law.** A controller parameterized by an $n$-vector $\theta_c$ produces:

$$
u(t) = \theta_c^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)
$$

where $\omega(t) = [r(t),\, y(t),\, \dots]^{\top}$ is the **regressor vector** carrying measurable signals.

**Step 2 — Define the adaptation objective.** The standard MRAC goal is to make the closed-loop plant asymptotically match the reference model:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \left[ y(t) - y_m(t) \right] = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two equations.** Substituting the control law into the plant equation yields the *closed-loop* error dynamics:

$$
e(t) = W_m(s)[r(t)] - G_p(s)[\theta_c^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)]
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce parameter error.** Let $\theta^*$ be the *ideal* controller parameter vector that would produce perfect model matching. Define:

$$
\phi(t) = \theta_c(t) - \theta^*
$$

Then the closed-loop error can be written *linearly* in $\phi$:

$$
e(t) = -\rho \,\phi^{\top}(t)\,\xi(t)
$$

where $\rho > 0$ is a *matching scalar* and $\xi(t)$ is a measurable filtered regressor. This is the **standard MRAC error model**.

**Step 5 — Derive the MIT rule.** Define a quadratic cost:

$$
J(\theta_c) = \frac{1}{2} e^2(t)
$$

Steepest descent in $J$ with respect to $\theta_c$ gives:

$$
\dot{\theta}_c = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_c} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_c}
$$

This is the famous **MIT rule** used in early (1960s) MRAC designs.

**Step 6 — Derive the Lyapunov-based adaptation law.** Choose the candidate Lyapunov function:

$$
V = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma} \phi^{\top}\phi
$$

Differentiating along the trajectories and choosing the adaptation law to make $\dot{V} \le 0$:

$$
\dot{V} = e \dot{e} + \frac{1}{\gamma}\phi^{\top}\dot{\phi}
$$

Using the error model $e = -\rho\,\phi^{\top}\xi$ and selecting:

$$
\dot{\phi} = -\dot{\theta}_c = \gamma \rho \, e \, \xi
$$

yields:

$$
\dot{V} = -\rho \, e^2 \,\xi^{\top}\xi \le 0
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.** This guarantees **Lyapunov stability** of the adaptive system and **asymptotic tracking** under persistent excitation.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Adaptation Laws Compared

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a high-yield KTU comparison. A 14-mark question on "compare MRAC and STR" almost always requires filling this kind of table.

| Feature | MRAC | STR | Gain Scheduling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference model** | Explicit $W_m(s)$ | None (uses estimator) | None |
| **Identifier** | Implicit (via adaptation law) | Explicit (RLS/ML) | None |
| **Synthesis** | Direct from $\hat{\theta}$ | Pole placement, MV, GPC | Pre-computed lookup |
| **Stability proof** | Lyapunov, Popov | ASTR, Passivity | Empirical tuning |
| **Computational load** | Low–moderate | High | Negligible |
| **Transient response** | Excellent, model-driven | Depends on estimator | Open-loop lookup |
| **Robustness** | Needs $\sigma$-modification | Needs covariance resetting | Sensitive to scheduling variable accuracy |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Adaptation of a Single-Gain Plant

> [!NOTE]
> **Plant:** $\dot{y} = -a\,y + b\,u$, with unknown $a > 0$, $b > 0$.
> **Reference model:** $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$, both $a_m, b_m$ known.
> **Goal:** Design an adaptive law so that $y \to y_m$.

**Step A — Set up the control law.** Let:

$$
u = \hat{\theta}_1 r - \hat{\theta}_2 y
$$

**Step B — Substitute into the plant.**

$$
\dot{y} = -a\,y + b(\hat{\theta}_1 r - \hat{\theta}_2 y) = -b\hat{\theta}_2\,y + b\hat{\theta}_1\,r - a\,y
$$

**Step C — Define the tracking error** $e = y - y_m$.

$$
\dot{e} = \dot{y} - \dot{y}_m
$$

**Step D — Identify ideal parameters** that achieve $\dot{y} = \dot{y}_m$:

$$
b\theta_1^* = b_m \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta_1^* = \frac{b_m}{b}
$$

$$
b\theta_2^* = a_m - a \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta_2^* = \frac{a_m - a}{b}
$$

**Step E — Define parameter error** $\tilde{\theta}_i = \theta_i^* - \hat{\theta}_i$.

**Step F — Apply Lyapunov-based update** using $V = \frac{1}{2}e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma}(\tilde{\theta}_1^2 + \tilde{\theta}_2^2)$:

$$
\dot{\hat{\theta}}_1 = \gamma\, e\, r, \qquad \dot{\hat{\theta}}_2 = \gamma\, e\, y
$$

**Step G — Verify stability.** $\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 \le 0$ guarantees asymptotic tracking.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Generic Adaptive Control System

The following Mermaid block captures every standard KTU textbook block diagram for adaptive control, including the two feedback loops — the **inner loop** (regulation) and the **outer loop** (adaptation).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    refSig["Reference r of t"] --> sumA(("+"))
    model["Reference Model W m of s"] --> errNode(("e = y - y m"))
    plant["Plant G p of s"]
    ctrl["Controller K of theta hat"]
    adapt["Adaptation / Parameter Update Law"]
    est["Parameter Estimator"]
    sumA --> ctrl
    ctrl -->|"u of t"| plant
    plant -->|"y of t"| errNode
    errNode -->|"error e"| adapt
    errNode -->|"error e"| ctrl
    plant --> est
    est -->|"theta hat of t"| adapt
    adapt -->|"updated gains"| ctrl
    model --> errNode
    errNode --> errOut["Tracking Error e of t"]

    style refSig fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style plant fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style ctrl fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    style adapt fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style est fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style model fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The diagram is a **dual-loop** structure. The **inner loop** (plant ↔ controller) is the standard regulation loop. The **outer loop** (plant → estimator → adaptation law → controller) is what makes the system *adaptive* — the controller literally redesigns itself each instant.

### 4.2 Classification Flowchart: Adaptive Control Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    adaptive["Adaptive Control Techniques"]
    adaptive --> gs["Gain Scheduling"]
    adaptive --> mrac["Model Reference Adaptive Control MRAC"]
    adaptive --> str["Self Tuning Regulators STR"]
    adaptive --> dual["Dual Control"]

    gs --> gs1["Lookup of gains vs scheduling variable"]
    gs --> gs2["No online estimation"]
    gs --> gs3["Fast, simple, open loop design"]

    mrac --> mrac1["Explicit reference model W m"]
    mrac --> mrac2["MIT rule or Lyapunov law"]
    mrac --> mrac3["Direct adaptation"]

    str --> str1["Online parameter estimator RLS or ML"]
    str --> str2["Indirect control synthesis"]
    str --> str3["Pole placement or minimum variance"]

    dual --> dual1["Joint probing and regulation"]
    dual --> dual2["Computationally intractable"]
    dual --> dual3["Theoretical benchmark only"]

    style adaptive fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style gs fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style mrac fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style str fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style dual fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Diagram: MRAC Single-Gain Adaptation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in["r of t"] --> m1["Reference Model W m"]
    m1 --> eNode(("minus"))
    p["Plant G p"] --> eNode
    eNode --> eOut["e of t"]
    eOut --> gain1["gamma"]
    eNode --> gain2["gamma"]
    p --> gain2
    gain1 --> theta1["theta 1 hat dot"]
    gain2 --> theta2["theta 2 hat dot"]
    theta1 --> ctrlK["Controller K"]
    theta2 --> ctrlK
    ctrlK -->|"u of t"| p

    style in fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style p fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style ctrlK fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    style m1 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** *Define adaptive control. List any two situations where it is preferred over conventional control.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Adaptive control** is a control strategy in which the controller parameters are automatically adjusted in real time to cope with unknown and/or time-varying plant dynamics, so as to maintain a desired closed-loop performance.
> *Situations:*
> (i) Aircraft autopilot flying through a wide range of altitudes and Mach numbers where aerodynamic coefficients drift.
> (ii) Chemical reactors with slow catalyst deactivation causing unknown gain drift.
> *(1 mark definition + 1 mark each situation = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** *Differentiate between gain scheduling and Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC).*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | Gain Scheduling | MRAC |
> |---|---|---|
> | Mechanism | Pre-computed gain tables indexed by operating point | Online adaptation law updates gains continuously |
> | Learning | None — open loop calibration | Yes — uses tracking error to update |
> | Robustness | Depends on accurate scheduling variable | Robust under persistent excitation |
> *(1 mark per correct differentiating pair, max 3)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** *Explain the three main building blocks of an adaptive control system with a neat block diagram.* **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Block 1 — Plant / Process Model (2 marks):**
> The plant is described by a parametric model such as $y(t) = \theta^{\top}\phi(t)$, where $\theta$ is the unknown parameter vector and $\phi(t)$ is the regressor. The plant is the system whose behaviour must be controlled.
>
> **Block 2 — Parameter Estimator / Identifier (2 marks):**
> An online algorithm (RLS, gradient, MIT rule) continuously updates the parameter estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$ using measurable input–output data $(u, y)$. The estimator converts raw measurements into a usable parametric model.
>
> **Block 3 — Control Law / Controller (2 marks):**
> Using the current $\hat{\theta}(t)$, the controller computes $u(t)$ to drive the plant so that its output tracks a desired reference. In MRAC, the control law is designed so that the closed-loop plant matches a stable reference model $W_m(s)$.
>
> **Block diagram** (1 mark) — as in Section 4.1 above, showing the dual-loop structure.

**(b)** *Derive the MIT rule for adjusting a single gain $\theta_c$ so that the plant output $y(t)$ tracks the reference model output $y_m(t)$.* **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Define tracking error (1 mark):**
> $$e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$$
>
> **Step 2 — Define quadratic cost (1 mark):**
> $$J(\theta_c) = \frac{1}{2} e^2(t)$$
>
> **Step 3 — Apply gradient descent (2 marks):**
> $$\dot{\theta}_c = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_c} = -\gamma \, e(t) \, \frac{\partial e(t)}{\partial \theta_c}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Specialize to single gain (2 marks):**
> If $e \approx -\theta_c \, y$, then $\partial e / \partial \theta_c = -y$, hence:
> $$\dot{\theta}_c = +\gamma \, e(t) \, y(t)$$
>
> **Step 5 — Limitation (1 mark):**
> MIT rule is a heuristic; it does not guarantee stability for large $\gamma$. Use Lyapunov-based law for provable stability.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** *Classify the various adaptive control schemes. Compare MRAC and Self-Tuning Regulator in detail.* **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Classification (3 marks):**
> Adaptive control is classified into:
> (i) **Gain Scheduling** — open-loop gain lookup.
> (ii) **MRAC** — model-reference based direct adaptation.
> (iii) **STR** — estimator + synthesis, indirect.
> (iv) **Dual Control** — joint learning and control.
>
> **Comparison (4 marks — table form):**
> | Criterion | MRAC | STR |
> |---|---|---|
> | Reference model | Explicit $W_m(s)$ | Not used |
> | Parameter estimate | Implicit in adaptation law | Explicit from RLS |
> | Design | Direct | Indirect |
> | Stability proof | Lyapunov / Popov | Passivity / ASTR |
> | Application | Flight control, robotics | Process industries |

**(b)** *Consider a first-order plant $\dot{y} = -a y + b u$ with unknown $a, b > 0$. Design a Lyapunov-based MRAC law so that $y$ tracks $y_m$ where $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$.* **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Control law structure (1 mark):** $u = \hat{\theta}_1 r - \hat{\theta}_2 y$.
>
> **Step 2 — Closed-loop dynamics (2 marks):**
> $$\dot{y} = (-a - b\hat{\theta}_2)y + b\hat{\theta}_1 r$$
>
> **Step 3 — Matching condition (1 mark):** ideal parameters $\theta_1^* = b_m/b$, $\theta_2^* = (a_m - a)/b$.
>
> **Step 4 — Error dynamics (1 mark):** $\dot{e} = -a_m e + b\tilde{\theta}_1 r - b\tilde{\theta}_2 y$.
>
> **Step 5 — Lyapunov-based adaptation (2 marks):** with $V = \frac{1}{2}e^2 + \frac{b}{2\gamma}(\tilde{\theta}_1^2 + \tilde{\theta}_2^2)$,
> $$\dot{\hat{\theta}}_1 = \gamma e r, \qquad \dot{\hat{\theta}}_2 = -\gamma e y$$
> giving $\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 \le 0$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing STR with MRAC** — STR has a *separate* estimator; MRAC does not. Mixing the two loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting persistent excitation** — Parameter convergence requires $\phi(t)$ to be *persistently exciting*. Stating this in the answer shows examiner awareness.
> 3. **MIT rule stability** — Many students present MIT rule as a finished solution. Examiners expect a follow-up: "MIT rule is not provably stable; use Lyapunov-based laws." Lose 1 mark if omitted.
> 4. **No mention of reference model** in MRAC answers — the reference model $W_m(s)$ is the *defining* element. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Sign errors in adaptation laws** — Mixing up $\dot{\hat{\theta}}$ vs $\dot{\tilde{\theta}}$ and forgetting the minus sign. Always derive from $\dot{V} \le 0$.
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark "explain the building blocks" sub-parts — at least 1 mark is reserved for the diagram.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Adaptive Control Introduction**
>
> - [x] Adaptive control = real-time *self-tuning* of controller parameters for unknown / time-varying plants.
> - [x] The **three core blocks** are: **Plant Model, Parameter Estimator, Control Law**.
> - [x] Classification: **Gain Scheduling, MRAC, STR, Dual Control**.
> - [x] **MRAC** uses an explicit reference model $W_m(s)$; the goal is $y \to y_m$.
> - [x] **STR** uses an explicit online estimator (RLS) + indirect synthesis (pole placement, MV).
> - [x] **Gain scheduling** is *not* a true learning system — it is a non-linear pre-compensated controller.
> - [x] **MIT rule:** $\dot{\theta}_c = -\gamma \, e \, \partial e / \partial \theta_c$. Heuristic, no guaranteed stability.
> - [x] **Lyapunov MRAC law:** $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = +\gamma \, e \, \phi$. Provably stable.
> - [x] **Persistent excitation** is a *necessary* condition for parameter convergence.
> - [x] **Stability** is established via Lyapunov, Passivity, or Input-to-State Stability (ISS).
> - [x] Real-world examples: aircraft autopilot, robotic manipulator, chemical reactor, EV motor drive.
> - [x] **Dual control** (Feldbaum) is the theoretical optimum — joint probing + regulation.
> - [x] Robust control ≠ adaptive control — robust fixes the controller at design time; adaptive updates online.
> - [x] Always include the **block diagram** in 7-mark questions — reserve 1 mark for it.
> - [x] Always mention **persistent excitation** in any convergence argument.
> - [x] Always derive adaptation law from a **Lyapunov function** when asked for a "stable" or "guaranteed" design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Block Diagram** is a graphical, functional representation of a dynamic system in which the major functional elements (subsystems, transfer functions, summing junctions, and signal paths) are represented by labelled **blocks** interconnected by directed **signal lines** (arrows) that denote the unidirectional flow of signals. Each block represents the **mathematical operation** (most commonly a transfer function) performed by that subsystem on its input signal to produce its output signal.

In the context of **Adaptive Control**, the block diagram is the primary pedagogical and analytical tool used to depict:
- The structure of the **plant** under control.
- The interaction between the **controller**, **identifier**, and **adjustment mechanism**.
- The architecture of reference-model adaptive systems (MRAS) and self-tuning regulators (STR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** A block diagram is *not* a circuit diagram or a wiring schematic. It is a **mathematical operator diagram** — every line is a *signal variable* (a function of time or frequency), and every block is an *operator* acting on that signal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **factory assembly line** where raw material enters Station A, gets partially processed, moves to Station B for further refinement, then to Station C for final finishing, and finally exits as a finished product.

- Each **station** is a *block* that performs a specific transformation.
- The **conveyor belts** carrying material between stations are *signal lines* with directional arrows.
- The **input** is raw material, the **output** is the finished product.
- A **quality inspector** stationed along the line who measures the output and shouts back corrections to Station B is exactly analogous to a **feedback loop** in a block diagram.

In an adaptive control system, the "quality inspector" is not just a fixed feedback path — it is an intelligent block that **continuously redesigns the controller** based on the plant's observed behaviour. This is what makes the block diagrams in adaptive control richer than those in classical control.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Block | Rectangular box representing a transfer function $G(s)$ |
| Signal Line | Directed arrow carrying a signal variable $X(s)$ |
| Summing Junction | Circular node where signals are added/subtracted |
| Take-off Point | Branching node where a signal splits into multiple parallel paths |
| Forward Path | Path from input to output travelling only in the arrow direction |
| Feedback Path | Path from output (or an intermediate node) back toward the input |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard convention: **Signals** are denoted by uppercase letters in the Laplace domain ($R(s)$, $Y(s)$, $E(s)$, $U(s)$) and by lowercase letters in the time domain ($r(t)$, $y(t)$, $e(t)$, $u(t)$). The independent variable is $s$ (complex frequency), not $j\omega$, when working with transfer functions.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Block Diagram — The Four Atomic Elements

Every block diagram, no matter how complex, is constructed from exactly **four** atomic graphical elements. Mastering these is the single highest-ROI activity for the Module 1 board exam.

**(a) The Block (Transfer Function Block)**

A rectangular block with the transfer function $G(s)$ written inside. The arrow enters from the left (input $X(s)$) and exits to the right (output $Y(s)$).

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot X(s)$$

The fundamental algebraic relation of a single block.

**(b) The Summing Junction ($\Sigma$-junction)**

A small circle with two or more incoming arrows and exactly one outgoing arrow. Each incoming arrow has an associated sign ($\,+\,$ or $\,-\,$) written next to it. The outgoing signal is the algebraic sum of the incoming signals.

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)$$

A summing junction is the **only** place where signals can be combined.

**(c) The Take-off Point (Branch Point)**

A solid dot placed on a signal line from which one or more additional signal lines branch off, all carrying the **same signal value**. No mathematical operation is performed.

**(d) The Signal Line (Arrowed Line)**

A directed line indicating the unidirectional flow of a signal from one element to another.

### 2.2 Elementary Block Diagram Reduction Rules

When the controller, plant, and feedback elements appear as separate blocks, the KTU examiner expects you to **collapse** them into a single equivalent transfer function using the rules below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked to "find the closed-loop transfer function", you are expected to **show the reduction step-by-step**, block-by-block, not jump to the final formula. Each simplification earns partial credit.

**Rule 1 — Cascade (Series) Connection**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot G_3(s)$$

**Rule 2 — Parallel Connection** (both blocks share the same input, outputs are summed)

$$\frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$$

**Rule 3 — Negative Feedback Loop**

For a forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$ with a negative summing junction:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Rule 4 — Positive Feedback Loop**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$$

**Rule 5 — Moving a Take-off Point Ahead of a Block**

If a take-off point is moved *ahead* of block $G(s)$, the branched signal must be multiplied by $1/G(s)$.

**Rule 6 — Moving a Summing Junction Ahead of a Block**

Moving a summing junction *ahead* of block $G(s)$ requires the parallel-additive signal to be pre-multiplied by $1/G(s)$ (i.e., divided by $G$).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Equivalent Transfer Function | Typical Use |
|:--:|:--|:--:|:--|
| 1 | Single block | $Y/X = G(s)$ | Basic plant model |
| 2 | Cascade of $n$ blocks | $Y/X = \prod_{i=1}^{n} G_i(s)$ | Series compensator + plant |
| 3 | Parallel sum | $Y/X = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$ | Feed-forward + feedback paths |
| 4 | Unity negative feedback | $Y/R = G/(1+G)$ | Standard servo loop |
| 5 | Non-unity negative feedback | $Y/R = G/(1+GH)$ | Sensor-scaled feedback |
| 6 | Positive feedback | $Y/R = G/(1-GH)$ | Regenerative / oscillator |
| 7 | Mason's path gain (general) | $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\sum_{k} P_k \Delta_k$ | Multi-loop systems |
| 8 | Loop gain | $L(s) = G(s)H(s)$ | Stability determinant |
| 9 | Characteristic polynomial | $\Delta(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Roots determine stability |
| 10 | Error signal (non-unity FB) | $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | Tracking error analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula sheet, the absolute-value bars around quantities (for example, the magnitude of a transfer function evaluated on the $j\omega$ axis) are typeset as $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ — written with `\vert` rather than `\|` so that KTU markdown table parsers do not break the column layout.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Block diagrams are not merely classroom tools. They are the **lingua franca** of practising control engineers because they:

- Decouple **structure** (signal flow architecture) from **values** (numerical parameters), enabling rapid redesign.
- Allow rapid computation of **closed-loop stability** by inspection of the characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$.
- Form the schematic backbone of **MATLAB/Simulink** models — every Simulink block corresponds directly to a block-diagram element.
- Enable the use of **Mason's Gain Formula** for arbitrarily complex multi-loop systems without manual reduction.

In **adaptive control**, the block diagram must additionally expose the *adaptation loop* — a second, slower loop that adjusts the parameters of the primary controller based on observed performance. This nested structure is unique to adaptive systems and is a Module 1 focal point.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Negative Feedback, Non-Unity)

We begin with the canonical negative-feedback block diagram: reference $R(s)$ enters a summing junction with positive sign, the feedback signal $H(s)Y(s)$ enters with negative sign, the error $E(s)$ drives the forward block $G(s)$, and the output $Y(s)$ is fed back through $H(s)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equations at each node.**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)$$

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the second equation into the first.**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot \bigl[G(s) \cdot E(s)\bigr]$$

$$E(s) = R(s) - G(s)H(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Collect terms in $E(s)$ on the left-hand side.**

$$E(s) + G(s)H(s) \cdot E(s) = R(s)$$

$$E(s)\,\bigl[1 + G(s)H(s)\bigr] = R(s)$$

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Step 4 — Recover $Y(s)$ using $Y(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$.**

$$Y(s) = \frac{G(s) \cdot R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Step 5 — Write the closed-loop transfer function in standard form.**

$$\boxed{\,T(s) \;=\; \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\,}$$

This is the **single most frequently quoted result** in Module 1. Memorise its derivation; it will appear verbatim (or with a sign change for positive feedback) in nearly every Part A and Part B question.

### 3.2 Derivation of Mason's Gain Formula (Path-Gain Method)

For a system with $N$ forward paths and $k$ individual loops, Mason's formula gives the overall transfer function in one symbolic step:

$$T(s) = \frac{1}{\Delta(s)} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k(s) \cdot \Delta_k(s)$$

where each symbol has a precise meaning, derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Define the Forward Path Gain $P_k$.**

A forward path is any continuous path from the input node to the output node that does not cross any node more than once. The forward path gain $P_k$ is the **product of all branch transmittances** encountered along that path.

**Step 2 — Define the Loop Gain $L_i$.**

A loop is a closed path that starts and ends at the same node without crossing any node twice. The loop gain $L_i$ is the product of branch transmittances around that loop.

**Step 3 — Define the Determinant of the Signal Flow Graph $\Delta(s)$.**

$$\Delta(s) = 1 - \sum_i L_i + \sum_{i,j} L_i L_j - \sum_{i,j,k} L_i L_j L_k + \cdots$$

In words: $\Delta$ is $1$ minus the sum of all individual loop gains, plus the sum of products of **two non-touching** loops, minus the sum of products of **three non-touching** loops, and so on. Two loops are *non-touching* if they share no common node.

**Step 4 — Define the Cofactor $\Delta_k(s)$ for the $k$-th Forward Path.**

$\Delta_k$ is the value of the graph determinant **after removing all loops that touch the $k$-th forward path**.

**Step 5 — Combine into Mason's Result.**

$$T(s) = \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k(s)\,\Delta_k(s)}{\Delta(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention in Mason's Formula:** Loop gains $L_i$ are entered with their *natural sign* — a positive sign if the loop contains an even number of negative summing junctions, and a negative sign otherwise. The alternating $\pm$ signs in the $\Delta$ expansion then handle the bookkeeping algebraically.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Multi-Loop Reduction by Mason's Formula

Consider the block diagram below (described textually since it is reduced symbolically):

- Two forward paths from $R(s)$ to $Y(s)$:
  - $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$
  - $P_2 = G_4$
- Three individual loops:
  - $L_1 = -G_1 G_2 H_1$ (inner loop)
  - $L_2 = -G_2 G_3 H_2$ (middle loop)
  - $L_3 = -G_1 G_2 G_3 H_3$ (outer loop, sharing nodes with $L_1$ and $L_2$)
- Non-touching loop pair: *none* (every loop touches every other loop because they share nodes $G_2$).

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta$.**

$$\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) + (\text{non-touching pairs}) - \cdots$$

$$\Delta = 1 - \bigl(-G_1G_2H_1 - G_2G_3H_2 - G_1G_2G_3H_3\bigr) + 0$$

$$\Delta = 1 + G_1G_2H_1 + G_2G_3H_2 + G_1G_2G_3H_3$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cofactor for $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$.**

Forward path $P_1$ touches *all* three loops, so all loops must be removed:

$$\Delta_1 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cofactor for $P_2 = G_4$.**

Forward path $P_2$ touches all loops as well (assuming it shares the input/output node):

$$\Delta_2 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Assemble Mason's Transfer Function.**

$$T(s) = \frac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta}$$

$$\boxed{\,T(s) \;=\; \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3 + G_4}{1 + G_1G_2H_1 + G_2G_3H_2 + G_1G_2G_3H_3}\,}$$

This single-line answer replaces what would otherwise be a three-to-four-step manual reduction. Mason's formula is therefore the most efficient tool for any multi-loop block diagram encountered in the KTU board examination.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reduction Rule Application Map

| Situation | Permissible Operation | Resulting Block | Sign of Operation |
|:--|:--|:--:|:--:|
| Two blocks in series | Multiply transmittances | $G_1 G_2$ | $\times$ |
| Two blocks in parallel | Add transmittances | $G_1 + G_2$ | $+$ |
| Negative feedback loop | $G / (1+GH)$ | Single block | Division |
| Positive feedback loop | $G / (1-GH)$ | Single block | Division |
| Moving summing junction before block | Pre-divide adder input by $G$ | $E/G + R$ | $\div$ |
| Moving summing junction after block | Post-multiply adder input by $G$ | $E \cdot G + R$ | $\times$ |
| Moving take-off point before block | Branch signal $\div G$ | $X/G$ | $\div$ |
| Moving take-off point after block | Branch signal $\times G$ | $X \cdot G$ | $\times$ |
| Block on a feedback path | Treat feedback path as a block $H$ | Use Rule 3 or 4 | — |
| Multiple non-touching loops in Mason | $1 + L_iL_j$ term | $\Delta$ expansion | $+$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Negative-Feedback Block Diagram (Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refNode[R s] -->|r of t| sumA(("+"))
    fbNode[H s] -->|minus sign| sumA(("+"))
    sumA --> errNode[E s]
    errNode --> plantNode[G s]
    plantNode --> outNode[Y s]
    outNode --> sensorNode[H s]
    sensorNode --> fbNode
    style refNode fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style outNode fill:#b0ffd0,stroke:#333
    style sumA fill:#ffd6b0,stroke:#333
    style plantNode fill:#b0d0ff,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide for the Diagram Above**

- The reference input $R(s)$ enters the summing junction with a positive sign.
- The feedback signal $H(s) \cdot Y(s)$ enters the summing junction with a negative sign.
- The output of the summing junction is the tracking error $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Y(s)$.
- The error drives the forward-path block $G(s)$, whose output is $Y(s)$.
- The take-off point on $Y(s)$ simultaneously feeds the output node and the feedback sensor block $H(s)$.

### 4.2 Multi-Loop Block Diagram with Inner and Outer Loops (Suitable for Mason's Formula)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Rin[R s] --> s1(("+"))
    s1 --> gA[G1 s]
    gA --> s2(("+"))
    s2 --> gB[G2 s]
    gB --> s3(("+"))
    s3 --> gC[G3 s]
    gC --> Yout[Y s]
    gA --> s4(("+"))
    s4 --> hA[H1 s]
    hA --> s2
    gC --> s5(("+"))
    s5 --> hB[H2 s]
    hB --> s3
    Yout --> hC[H3 s]
    hC --> s1
    Rin --> gD[G4 s]
    gD --> Yout
    style Rin fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style Yout fill:#b0ffd0,stroke:#333
    style gA fill:#b0d0ff,stroke:#333
    style gB fill:#b0d0ff,stroke:#333
    style gC fill:#b0d0ff,stroke:#333
    style gD fill:#b0d0ff,stroke:#333
    style s1 fill:#ffd6b0,stroke:#333
    style s2 fill:#ffd6b0,stroke:#333
    style s3 fill:#ffd6b0,stroke:#333
    style s4 fill:#ffd6b0,stroke:#333
    style s5 fill:#ffd6b0,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide for the Multi-Loop Diagram**

- **Forward Path 1** runs along the top: $R \to G_1 \to G_2 \to G_3 \to Y$, giving $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$.
- **Forward Path 2** is the bottom bypass: $R \to G_4 \to Y$, giving $P_2 = G_4$.
- **Loop 1** ($L_1$): $G_1 \to$ summing junction $\to G_2 \to H_1 \to$ back to $G_1$, with a negative feedback sign, hence $L_1 = -G_1 G_2 H_1$.
- **Loop 2** ($L_2$): $G_2 \to G_3 \to H_2 \to$ back, giving $L_2 = -G_2 G_3 H_2$.
- **Loop 3** ($L_3$): the outer loop $G_1 G_2 G_3 H_3$ gives $L_3 = -G_1 G_2 G_3 H_3$.

### 4.3 Adaptive Control Block Diagram — Reference Model Adaptive System (MRAS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refIn[R s] --> comp(("+"))
    plantOut[Y s] -->|feedback signal| comp(("+"))
    comp --> ctrlErr[E s]
    ctrlErr --> ctrlNode[Controller C s]
    ctrlNode --> ctrlU[U s]
    ctrlU --> plantNode[Plant Gp s]
    plantNode --> plantOut
    refIn --> refModel[Reference Model M s]
    refModel --> refOut[Ym s]
    plantOut --> adaptErr(("+"))
    refOut -->|negative sign| adaptErr(("+"))
    adaptErr --> adaptLaw[Adaptation Law f s]
    adaptLaw -->|parameter update| ctrlNode
    style refIn fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style plantOut fill:#b0ffd0,stroke:#333
    style refOut fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#333
    style ctrlNode fill:#b0d0ff,stroke:#333
    style adaptLaw fill:#e6b0ff,stroke:#333
    style plantNode fill:#b0d0ff,stroke:#333
    style refModel fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide for the MRAS Adaptive Diagram**

- The **inner loop** (cyan/blue) is the classical servo loop: error $E(s)$ drives controller $C(s)$, which commands the plant $G_p(s)$.
- The **outer adaptation loop** (purple) is unique to adaptive systems: the adaptation-law block $f(\cdot)$ continuously compares the plant output $Y(s)$ with the reference-model output $Y_m(s)$ and adjusts the parameters of $C(s)$ online.
- Two summing junctions are visible: one for the servo error $E(s)$ and one for the adaptation error $E_a(s)$.
- This nested structure cannot be analysed by classical single-loop reduction; Module 2 onwards extends block-diagram tools (Lyapunov stability, MIT rule, etc.) to handle such nested adaptation loops rigorously.

### 4.4 Block-to-Signal-Flow-Graph Conversion Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blkIn[Input node Xi] -->|branch gain a| blkMid[Intermediate node Xj]
    blkMid -->|transmittance G| blkOut[Output node Xk]
    style blkIn fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style blkMid fill:#b0d0ff,stroke:#333
    style blkOut fill:#b0ffd0,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide**

- A block $G$ from input $X_i$ to output $X_j$ becomes a single directed branch from node $X_i$ to node $X_j$ with transmittance $G$.
- A summing junction becomes a node with multiple incoming branches, each with its own sign-encoded transmittance.
- A take-off point becomes a single node from which multiple outgoing branches emerge, each carrying transmittance $1$.
- This one-to-one correspondence allows Mason's gain formula to be applied to any block diagram once it has been redrawn as a signal-flow graph.
