> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stochastic Self Tuning Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)** is an adaptive controller that combines on-line parameter estimation of a stochastic (noise-corrupted) dynamical plant with a recursively re-tuned control law, such that the closed-loop system asymptotically approaches the optimal stochastic regulator performance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the STR is built on three pillars:

1. A **stochastic plant model** — typically the **ARMAX (Auto-Regressive Moving Average with eXogenous input)** structure.
2. An **on-line parameter estimator** — most commonly the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** algorithm (or its variants like RLS with forgetting factor / Kalman filter / stochastic approximation).
3. A **control design block** — most commonly the **Minimum Variance Controller (MVC)** or **Generalized Minimum Variance (GMV)** controller, whose parameters are updated at every sampling instant using the latest plant parameter estimates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The stochastic STR is the cornerstone of KTU Module 3 because it relaxes the deterministic assumption of Module 2 (Model Reference Adaptive Control) and explicitly accounts for **colored measurement noise**, **stochastic disturbances**, and **uncertain plant parameters**.

Mathematically, a generic stochastic SISO plant is represented as:

$$
A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = B(z^{-1})\,u(t-k) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$

where $\omega(t)$ is a **zero-mean white noise** sequence with variance $\sigma^{2}$, $k$ is the inherent **time delay**, and $u(t), y(t)$ are the input and output respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Noisy Cruise Control:**
> Imagine driving on a Kerala highway with cruise control engaged. The road has bumps, the wind gusts, and the speedometer has a small jittery noise overlay. A **deterministic** adaptive controller would pretend the speed reading is exact and aggressively re-tune the throttle. A **stochastic** self-tuning regulator, however, *knows* the speed signal is noisy. It:
> (a) first **filters / estimates** the true speed by averaging past noisy readings (parameter estimation),
> (b) then **recomputes** the throttle setting (control law) assuming its estimates are the true values (certainty equivalence),
> (c) and does this every fraction of a second.
>
> The result: smoother, fuel-efficient driving that converges to the target speed despite the noise — exactly what a stochastic STR does for industrial processes.

### 1.3 Why Stochastic STR is Important

- Handles **realistic industrial environments** where noise is unavoidable (sensor quantization, actuator drift, load disturbances).
- The **Minimum Variance** objective is mathematically tractable in ARMAX form, leading to a clean, implementable control law.
- Foundation for **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** adaptive variants, **self-tuning predictors**, and **adaptive Kalman filtering**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key metric for KTU:** The performance benchmark for any stochastic STR is the theoretical **minimum output variance** $J_{\min} = E\{y^{2}(t+k)\}$, which is achieved only when plant parameters are known exactly. The STR attempts to reach this bound asymptotically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of a Stochastic STR

A stochastic STR can be decomposed into three interacting subsystems operating at every sampling instant $t$:

- **Block A — Plant (ARMAX form):**
  $$
  A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-k}\,u(t) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
  $$

- **Block B — Recursive Parameter Estimator (e.g., RLS):**
  Uses past input–output data to produce an estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$ of the unknown parameter vector $\theta$.

- **Block C — Control Law Synthesizer (e.g., Minimum Variance or GMV):**
  Uses $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to compute the control signal $u(t)$ that minimizes the chosen cost function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Certainty Equivalence Principle:** When the estimator is replaced by its converged estimate, the STR behaves as if the true parameters are known. This principle is what allows the design to be *separable* into an estimation problem and a control problem.

### 2.2 The ARMAX Plant Model (Detailed)

For an SISO system with delay $k \geq 1$:

$$
A(z^{-1}) = 1 + a_{1}z^{-1} + a_{2}z^{-2} + \cdots + a_{n_{a}}z^{-n_{a}}
$$

$$
B(z^{-1}) = b_{0} + b_{1}z^{-1} + \cdots + b_{n_{b}}z^{-n_{b}}
$$

$$
C(z^{-1}) = 1 + c_{1}z^{-1} + c_{2}z^{-2} + \cdots + c_{n_{c}}z^{-n_{c}}
$$

- The **$A$ polynomial** captures the output's auto-regressive dynamics.
- The **$B$ polynomial** captures the actuator-to-output path (including any zero-order hold effect).
- The **$C$ polynomial** models the **colored noise** that drives the system. When $C(z^{-1}) = 1$, the noise is white and the model collapses to ARX.

### 2.3 The Minimum Variance Control (MVC) Law

For the ARMAX plant, the **$k$-step ahead optimal predictor** of $y(t+k)$ is:

$$
\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,F(z^{-1})\,u(t) + G(z^{-1})\,y(t)
$$

where $F(z^{-1})$ and $G(z^{-1})$ are the unique polynomials obtained from the **Diophantine identity**:

$$
C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1}) + z^{-k}\,G(z^{-1})
$$

with $F$ of degree $k-1$ and $G$ of degree $n_{a}-1$.

The **Minimum Variance Control law** is obtained by setting the predicted output to zero:

$$
u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})}\,y(t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $k$-step ahead?** In the stochastic case, only the $k$-step ahead prediction is independent of future noise $\omega(t), \omega(t+1), \ldots, \omega(t+k-1)$. Thus the prediction error variance $E\{[y(t+k) - \hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t)]^{2}\}$ becomes the unavoidable lower bound on output variance.

### 2.4 Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Estimator

The unknown parameter vector is:

$$
\theta = \begin{bmatrix} a_{1} & a_{2} & \cdots & a_{n_{a}} & b_{0} & b_{1} & \cdots & b_{n_{b}} & c_{1} & c_{2} & \cdots & c_{n_{c}} \end{bmatrix}^{T}
$$

with corresponding regression vector $\varphi(t-1)$. The RLS update law is:

$$
\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t)\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\hat{\theta}(t-1)\bigr]
$$

$$
K(t) = \frac{P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}
$$

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\Bigl[I - K(t)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)\Bigr]\,P(t-1)
$$

where $0 < \lambda \leq 1$ is the **forgetting factor** (commonly $0.95 \leq \lambda \leq 1$).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | KTU-Standard Form |
|---|---|---|
| Plant | Stochastic SISO ARMAX | $A y(t) = B z^{-k} u(t) + C \omega(t)$ |
| Noise | Zero-mean white | $\omega(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$, **$\sigma^{2}$ = noise variance** |
| Diophantine identity | Defines MVC polynomials | $C = A F + z^{-k} G$ with $\deg F = k-1$ |
| Minimum variance cost | Performance index | $J = E\{y^{2}(t+k)\}$ |
| Minimum cost value | Achievable bound | $J_{\min} = \sigma^{2}\bigl(1 + f_{1}^{2} + f_{2}^{2} + \cdots + f_{k-1}^{2}\bigr)$ |
| Control law | MVC for known plant | $u(t) = -G\,y(t) / (B\,F)$ |
| STR control law | MVC with estimates | $u(t) = -\hat{G}\,y(t) / (\hat{B}\,\hat{F})$ |
| RLS gain | Update weight | $K(t) = P(t-1)\varphi / (\lambda + \varphi^{T} P \varphi)$ |
| Covariance update | Forgetting factor form | $P(t) = [I - K \varphi^{T}] P(t-1) / \lambda$ |
| Stability condition | Persistent excitation | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} \varphi(t)\,\varphi^{T}(t) \succ 0$ |
| Convergence condition | Projected RLS | $\Vert \hat{\theta}(t) - \theta \Vert \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Stochastic STRs are deployed in **paper mills** (basis weight control), **petrochemical reactors** (composition control with noisy analyzers), and **autonomous vehicles** (lane-keeping under sensor noise). The MVC cost $J_{\min}$ is the irreducible variance — the controller cannot reduce output variance below this, even with perfect knowledge of parameters.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries:** Adaptive control of distillation columns, heat exchangers, and CSTRs where stochastic disturbances dominate.
- **Aerospace:** Adaptive autopilots operating in turbulent air (modeled as stochastic disturbances).
- **Robotics:** Joint-position control with noisy encoder feedback.
- **Econometrics / Finance:** Adaptive forecasting of stochastic time series using the same ARMAX/RLS toolbox.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Variance Predictor

**Step 1 — Start from the ARMAX plant model.**

$$
A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-k}\,u(t) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$

Rearranging for $y(t+k)$:

$$
y(t+k) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,u(t) + \frac{C(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,\omega(t+k)
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose $C/A$ into a $k$-step-ahead predictable part and a moving-average remainder.**

Since the noise is white, $\omega(t+1), \omega(t+2), \ldots, \omega(t+k)$ are *not* known at time $t$, but $\omega(t), \omega(t-1), \ldots$ are. We therefore divide $C(z^{-1})$ by $A(z^{-1})$ to obtain:

$$
\frac{C(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})} = F(z^{-1}) + z^{-k}\,\frac{G(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}
$$

where $\deg F = k-1$ and $\deg G = n_{a}-1$. Multiplying through by $A(z^{-1})$ gives the **Diophantine identity**:

$$
C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1}) + z^{-k}\,G(z^{-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the output expression.**

$$
y(t+k) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,u(t) + F(z^{-1})\,\omega(t+k) + z^{-k}\,\frac{G(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,\omega(t+k)
$$

The last term has $z^{-k}$, meaning it depends on $\omega(t), \omega(t-1), \ldots$ — values that *are* known at time $t$. The middle term $F(z^{-1})\,\omega(t+k)$ depends on $\omega(t+k), \omega(t+k-1), \ldots, \omega(t+1)$, which are *unknown*.

**Step 4 — Identify the optimal predictor.**

The **$k$-step-ahead minimum-variance predictor** is the part that depends only on known quantities:

$$
\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,u(t) + z^{-k}\,\frac{G(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})}\,\omega(t+k)
$$

But $\omega$ itself is unknown; using $A\,\omega = A\,y - B\,z^{-k}\,u$ (from the plant equation) we rewrite:

$$
\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{B(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,F(z^{-1})\,u(t) + \frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,y(t)
$$

This is the standard form shown in Section 2.

**Step 5 — Compute the prediction error and its variance.**

The prediction error is:

$$
\tilde{y}(t+k \mid t) = y(t+k) - \hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = F(z^{-1})\,\omega(t+k)
$$

Since $\omega$ is white with variance $\sigma^{2}$:

$$
E\{\tilde{y}^{2}(t+k \mid t)\} = \sigma^{2}\bigl(1 + f_{1}^{2} + f_{2}^{2} + \cdots + f_{k-1}^{2}\bigr) = J_{\min}
$$

This is the **irreducible minimum variance** of the closed-loop output — the lower bound that no controller can beat.

**Step 6 — Derive the minimum variance control law.**

The control objective is $J = E\{y^{2}(t+k)\}$. The optimal choice at each instant is to set $\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t) = 0$, which gives:

$$
\frac{B(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,F(z^{-1})\,u(t) + \frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})\,}\,y(t) = 0
$$

Solving for $u(t)$ yields the **MVC law**:

$$
u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})}\,y(t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The polynomials $F$ and $G$ are obtained by long division of $C$ by $A$, carried out exactly to $k-1$ quotient terms plus a $z^{-k}$ remainder. This is the **Diophantine equation** solution — a high-yield KTU exam item.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Update

**Step 1 — Stack the ARMAX equation into a linear regression form.**

Define the regression vector at instant $t$:

$$
\varphi^{T}(t) = \bigl[-y(t), -y(t-1), \ldots, -y(t-n_{a}+1),\,u(t-k),\ldots,u(t-k-n_{b}+1),\,\omega(t),\ldots\bigr]
$$

and the parameter vector:

$$
\theta^{T} = \bigl[a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n_{a}},\,b_{0}, b_{1}, \ldots, b_{n_{b}},\,c_{1}, c_{2}, \ldots, c_{n_{c}}\bigr]
$$

Then the plant model is compactly:

$$
y(t) = \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\theta + \omega(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the off-line least-squares cost.**

$$
V_{N}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^{N}\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\theta\bigr]^{2}
$$

Setting $\partial V_{N}/\partial \theta = 0$ gives:

$$
\Bigl[\sum_{t=1}^{N}\varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)\Bigr]\,\theta = \sum_{t=1}^{N}\varphi(t-1)\,y(t)
$$

The off-line solution is $\theta_{N} = P_{N}^{-1}\,b_{N}$ where $P_{N} = \sum \varphi\varphi^{T}$ and $b_{N} = \sum \varphi y$.

**Step 3 — Introduce the forgetting factor.**

For time-varying plants, weight recent data more heavily:

$$
V_{N}^{\lambda}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-t}\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\theta\bigr]^{2}, \qquad 0 < \lambda \leq 1
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to recursive form.**

Define:

$$
P^{-1}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\lambda^{t-i}\,\varphi(i-1)\,\varphi^{T}(i-1)
$$

Then:

$$
P^{-1}(t) = \lambda\,P^{-1}(t-1) + \varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)
$$

Applying the **matrix inversion lemma** $(A + uv^{T})^{-1} = A^{-1} - \dfrac{A^{-1}uv^{T}A^{-1}}{1 + v^{T}A^{-1}u}$ with $A = \lambda P^{-1}(t-1)$ and $u = v = \varphi(t-1)$ yields:

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\Bigl[I - \frac{P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}\Bigr]\,P(t-1)
$$

Defining the **gain vector**:

$$
K(t) = \frac{P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,P(t-1)\,\varphi(t-1)}
$$

the parameter update becomes:

$$
\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t)\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\hat{\theta}(t-1)\bigr]
$$

The bracketed term is the **prediction error** $e(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t \mid t-1)$, which is measurable online.

### 3.3 The Combined STR Algorithm (Implementation)

At each sampling instant $t$, the STR executes the following steps in order:

**Step 1 — Measure** $y(t)$.
**Step 2 — Form** the regression vector $\varphi(t-1)$.
**Step 3 — Predict** using current estimates: $\hat{y}(t) = \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\hat{\theta}(t-1)$.
**Step 4 — Compute** the prediction error: $e(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t)$.
**Step 5 — Update** parameters using the RLS formulas.
**Step 6 — Solve** the Diophantine identity $\hat{C} = \hat{A}\hat{F} + z^{-k}\hat{G}$ with the *new* estimates.
**Step 7 — Compute** the control signal:

$$
u(t) = -\frac{\hat{G}(z^{-1})}{\hat{B}(z^{-1})\,\hat{F}(z^{-1})}\,y(t)
$$

**Step 8 — Apply** $u(t)$ to the plant, increment $t \to t+1$, and loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Diophantine identity in Step 6 must be re-solved *at every* sampling instant with the freshly updated $\hat{A}, \hat{C}$. Students often forget this and use a fixed pre-computed $F, G$, which destroys adaptivity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r t] --> SUMC[Sum Node Controller]
    Y[Plant Output y t] --> SUMC
    SUMC --> E[Error e t]
    E --> CTRL[Control Law MVC Block]
    CTRL --> UT[Control Signal u t]
    UT --> PLANT[(ARMAX Plant)]
    PLANT --> YT[Output y t]
    PLANT --> WT[White Noise w t]

    Y --> RLS[RLS Parameter Estimator]
    UT --> RLS
    RLS --> THETA[Estimated Parameters theta hat]
    THETA --> CTRL

    THETA --> DIO[Diophantine Solver F G]
    DIO --> CTRL

    YT --> YB[Output Buffer]
    UT --> UB[Input Buffer]
    YB --> RLS
    UB --> RLS
```

### 4.2 Information Flow Inside the RLS Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[New Measurement y t] --> ERR[Compute Prediction Error e t]
    PHI[Regression Vector phi t-1] --> ERR
    PCOV[Old Covariance P t-1] --> GAIN[Compute Kalman Gain K t]
    PHI --> GAIN
    PCOV --> GAIN
    GAIN --> UPD[Update theta hat t]
    THETAOLD[Old Estimate theta hat t-1] --> UPD
    ERR --> UPD
    UPD --> THETANEW[New Estimate theta hat t]
    UPD --> PNEW[New Covariance P t]
    PNEW --> PCOV
    THETANEW --> THETAOLD
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Interaction Between Plant, Estimator, and Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLANT_BLOCK[ARMAX Plant]
      AOUT[Output Channel A z-1]
      BOUT[Input Channel B z-1 z-k]
      NOUT[Noise Channel C z-1]
    end

    subgraph ESTIMATOR_BLOCK[Recursive Estimator]
      REGR[Regressor phi]
      RLS_ALGO[RLS Algorithm]
      PEST[Parameter Estimate]
    end

    subgraph CONTROLLER_BLOCK[Minimum Variance Controller]
      DIO[Diophantine Solver]
      MVC[Control Law u t]
    end

    BOUT --> AOUT
    NOUT --> AOUT
    AOUT --> REGR
    REGR --> RLS_ALGO
    RLS_ALGO --> PEST
    PEST --> DIO
    DIO --> MVC
    MVC --> BOUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams show three coupled loops operating at the same sampling rate: the **outer control loop** (plant $\rightarrow$ controller $\rightarrow$ plant), the **inner estimation loop** (plant outputs $\rightarrow$ RLS $\rightarrow$ parameter update), and the **adaptive law loop** (estimates $\rightarrow$ Diophantine $\rightarrow$ controller). The convergence of all three loops determines the success of the STR.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator. State the three main blocks of an STR and briefly explain the role of the certainty equivalence principle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator (STR) is an adaptive controller that adjusts its control law in real time based on online estimates of the parameters of a stochastic (noise-affected) plant, so as to drive the closed-loop output variance toward its theoretical minimum.

The three main blocks are:

1. **Parameter Estimator** (e.g., RLS) — estimates plant parameters $\hat{\theta}(t)$ from past input–output data.
2. **Control Design Block** (e.g., Minimum Variance Controller) — uses $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to design the current control law $u(t)$.
3. **Plant** (ARMAX form) — the actual stochastic process being controlled.

The **certainty equivalence principle** states that the control law computed using the *estimated* parameters $\hat{\theta}(t)$ is implemented as if $\hat{\theta}(t)$ were the *true* parameter $\theta$. This decouples estimation from control design, making the STR implementable.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Write the ARMAX model of a stochastic SISO plant and identify the role of each polynomial $A(z^{-1})$, $B(z^{-1})$ and $C(z^{-1})$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-k}\,u(t) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$

- **$A(z^{-1})$** — Autoregressive polynomial: encodes the *output-side dynamics* (poles, internal feedback) of the plant.
- **$B(z^{-1})$** — Input polynomial: encodes the *actuator-to-output* path, including zeros introduced by the input.
- **$C(z^{-1})$** — Noise polynomial: shapes the *colored disturbance* acting on the system. When $C(z^{-1}) = 1$, the disturbance reduces to pure white noise and the model collapses to ARX.
- $\omega(t)$ is a **zero-mean white noise** with variance $\sigma^{2}$, and $k$ is the **system delay** in samples.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern (ESE):** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either-Or choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — 7 marks]**
*Consider a stochastic SISO plant with delay $k = 1$ and ARMAX form:*
$$
(1 - 0.8\,z^{-1})\,y(t) = 0.5\,z^{-1}\,u(t) + (1 - 0.4\,z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$
*Derive the minimum-variance control law for this plant and compute the minimum achievable output variance $J_{\min}$ when the noise variance is $\sigma^{2} = 0.25$.*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the polynomials.**

$$
A(z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.8\,z^{-1}, \quad B(z^{-1}) = 0.5, \quad C(z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.4\,z^{-1}, \quad k = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the Diophantine identity with $k = 1$.**

Since $k = 1$, the polynomial $F(z^{-1})$ has degree $k - 1 = 0$, i.e. $F(z^{-1}) = f_{0}$ (a constant). Then:

$$
C = A\,F + z^{-1}\,G
$$

Substituting $A = 1 - 0.8 z^{-1}$, $F = f_{0}$, $C = 1 - 0.4 z^{-1}$:

$$
1 - 0.4 z^{-1} = (1 - 0.8 z^{-1})\,f_{0} + z^{-1}\,G(z^{-1})
$$

Equating constant terms: $f_{0} = 1$.
Equating $z^{-1}$ terms: $-0.4 = -0.8 f_{0} + g_{0} \Rightarrow g_{0} = -0.4 + 0.8(1) = 0.4$.

So:

$$
F(z^{-1}) = 1, \quad G(z^{-1}) = 0.4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the minimum variance.**

Since $F$ is a constant, $f_{0} = 1$ is the only coefficient. The minimum variance is:

$$
J_{\min} = \sigma^{2}\,(f_{0}^{2}) = 0.25 \times 1^{2} = 0.25
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the MVC control law.**

$$
u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})}\,y(t) = -\frac{0.4}{0.5 \times 1}\,y(t) = -0.8\,y(t)
$$

So the minimum-variance control law is $u(t) = -0.8\,y(t)$, and the minimum achievable output variance is $J_{\min} = 0.25$.

---

**(b) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — 7 marks]**
*For a self-tuning regulator using the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) estimator with forgetting factor $\lambda$, derive the parameter update law starting from the weighted least-squares cost. State the role of the forgetting factor and explain what happens if $\lambda = 1$.*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the cost.**

$$
V_{N}^{\lambda}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-t}\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\theta\bigr]^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the gradient to zero.**

$$
\frac{\partial V_{N}^{\lambda}}{\partial \theta} = -\sum_{t=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-t}\,\varphi(t-1)\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\theta\bigr] = 0
$$

This gives:

$$
\Bigl[\sum_{t=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-t}\,\varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)\Bigr]\theta = \sum_{t=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-t}\,\varphi(t-1)\,y(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Define $P(N)$ and use the matrix inversion lemma.**

$$
P^{-1}(N) = \sum_{t=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-t}\,\varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)
$$

Recursive expansion:

$$
P^{-1}(t) = \lambda\,P^{-1}(t-1) + \varphi(t-1)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)
$$

Applying the matrix inversion lemma:

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\Bigl[I - K(t)\,\varphi^{T}(t-1)\Bigr]\,P(t-1), \quad K(t) = \frac{P(t-1)\varphi(t-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,P(t-1)\varphi(t-1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the parameter update.**

$$
\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t)\,\bigl[y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\,\hat{\theta}(t-1)\bigr]
$$

**Step 5 — Role of the forgetting factor $\lambda$.**

- $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ weights recent data more heavily, allowing the estimator to track *time-varying* plant parameters.
- If $\lambda = 1$, all data are equally weighted — the estimator loses its ability to track slow parameter drifts (suitable only for time-invariant plants).
- If $\lambda \to 0$, only the most recent data point is used — the estimator becomes very noisy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — 7 marks]**
*Explain the certainty equivalence principle in the design of self-tuning regulators. With a block diagram, describe the architecture of an indirect stochastic self-tuning regulator based on minimum variance control.*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the principle.**

The **certainty equivalence principle** asserts that an adaptive controller designed by replacing the unknown plant parameters with their online estimates — and treating those estimates as if they were the true parameters — achieves asymptotically optimal performance when the estimates converge.

**Step 2 — Justify the principle.**

For the ARMAX plant, the optimal control law is:

$$
u^{*}(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1};\theta)}{B(z^{-1};\theta)\,F(z^{-1};\theta)}\,y(t)
$$

If $\hat{\theta}(t) \to \theta$ as $t \to \infty$, then:

$$
u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1};\hat{\theta}(t))}{B(z^{-1};\hat{\theta}(t))\,F(z^{-1};\hat{\theta}(t))}\,y(t) \to u^{*}(t)
$$

Hence the STR asymptotically approaches the optimal MVC.

**Step 3 — Block diagram (textual).**

```
            +---------+        +---------+        +----------+        +----------+
   r(t) -->|  Sum    |--e(t)->|  MVC    |--u(t)->|  ARMAX   |--y(t)->|  Output  |
            |  Node   |        |  Law    |        |  Plant   |        |          |
            +---------+        +---------+        +----------+        +----------+
                  ^                  ^                                    |
                  |                  |                                    |
                  |            +-----+-----+                              |
                  |            |  Estimate  |<------ y(t), u(t) ----------+
                  |            |   θ̂(t)    |
                  |            +-----------+
                  |                  ^
                  |            +-----+-----+
                  +------ y(t)  |   RLS    |
                                 |Estimator |
                                 +----------+
```

The MVC law block receives $\hat{\theta}(t)$ from the RLS estimator; the RLS estimator uses both $u(t)$ and $y(t)$ as inputs.

**Step 4 — Algorithm summary.**

At each step:
1. Read $y(t)$.
2. Compute prediction error $e(t) = y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t-1)\hat{\theta}(t-1)$.
3. Update $\hat{\theta}(t)$ via RLS.
4. Solve Diophantine $\hat{C} = \hat{A}\hat{F} + z^{-1}\hat{G}$.
5. Compute and apply $u(t) = -\hat{G}y(t)/(\hat{B}\hat{F})$.

---

**(b) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — 7 marks]**
*A stochastic plant is described by:*
$$
(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})\,y(t) = 0.4 z^{-2}\,u(t) + (1 + 0.3 z^{-1})\,\omega(t)
$$
*Design the minimum variance controller assuming the plant parameters are known. Express the control law in terms of $y(t)$.*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the polynomials.**

$$
A = 1 - 0.5 z^{-1},\quad B = 0.4,\quad C = 1 + 0.3 z^{-1},\quad k = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the Diophantine equation with $k = 2$.**

$\deg F = k - 1 = 1$, so $F(z^{-1}) = 1 + f_{1}z^{-1}$, and $\deg G = n_{a} - 1 = 0$, so $G(z^{-1}) = g_{0}$.

$$
C = A F + z^{-2} G
$$

$$
1 + 0.3 z^{-1} = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + f_{1} z^{-1}) + z^{-2}\,g_{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the right side.**

$$
(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + f_{1} z^{-1}) = 1 + f_{1} z^{-1} - 0.5 z^{-1} - 0.5 f_{1} z^{-2}
$$

$$
= 1 + (f_{1} - 0.5) z^{-1} - 0.5 f_{1} z^{-2}
$$

Adding $z^{-2} g_{0}$:

$$
1 + (f_{1} - 0.5) z^{-1} + (g_{0} - 0.5 f_{1}) z^{-2}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate coefficients with $C = 1 + 0.3 z^{-1} + 0 z^{-2}$.**

- $z^{0}$: $1 = 1$ ✓
- $z^{-1}$: $f_{1} - 0.5 = 0.3 \Rightarrow f_{1} = 0.8$
- $z^{-2}$: $g_{0} - 0.5(0.8) = 0 \Rightarrow g_{0} = 0.4$

**Step 5 — Write the control law.**

$$
u(t) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})}\,y(t) = -\frac{0.4}{0.4\,(1 + 0.8 z^{-1})}\,y(t) = -\frac{1}{1 + 0.8 z^{-1}}\,y(t)
$$

In time-domain form:

$$
u(t) = -0.8\,u(t-1) - y(t)
$$

This is the minimum variance control law for the given plant.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Stochastic STR:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Diophantine re-solve** *(up to -2 marks)*: Students often plug in pre-computed $F, G$ without updating them after parameter estimation. The whole point of the STR is to re-solve $C = AF + z^{-k}G$ at *every* sampling instant with the latest $\hat{A}, \hat{C}$.
> 2. **Confusing ARX and ARMAX** *(up to -1 mark)*: ARX assumes $C = 1$ (white noise only). If the problem mentions *colored* noise, an ARMAX model *must* be used and the Diophantine equation *must* include $C \neq 1$.
> 3. **Mis-applying the certainty equivalence principle** *(up to -2 marks)*: The principle applies only *asymptotically*. Writing "the STR is *always* optimal" is wrong; it is optimal *in the limit* as $\hat{\theta} \to \theta$.
> 4. **Inversion lemma errors** in RLS derivation *(up to -2 marks)*: Forgetting the $\lambda$ in the denominator of $P(t)$ is a frequent slip.
> 5. **Wrong degree constraints** in Diophantine: $F$ must have degree $k-1$ exactly, and $G$ must have degree $n_a - 1$ exactly. Swapping these leads to under-determined or over-determined systems.
> 6. **Forgetting the predictor form**: In some problems, students write the control law in terms of future output $\hat{y}^{*}(t+k \mid t)$ instead of the regressor $y(t)$. Always express $u(t)$ in terms of *current and past* $y$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stochastic STR** = (Online Parameter Estimator) + (Recomputed Control Law) for a noisy ARMAX plant.
- **Plant model**: ARMAX form $A y = B z^{-k} u + C \omega$ is the universal KTU template.
- **Diophantine identity**: $C = A F + z^{-k} G$ defines the MVC polynomials; $\deg F = k-1$, $\deg G = n_a - 1$.
- **Minimum variance control law**: $u(t) = -G y / (B F)$, with estimates $\hat{G}, \hat{B}, \hat{F}$ in the STR.
- **Minimum output variance**: $J_{\min} = \sigma^{2}(1 + f_{1}^{2} + \cdots + f_{k-1}^{2})$ — the irreducible lower bound.
- **RLS update** (with forgetting factor $\lambda$): gain $K(t) = P(t-1)\varphi/(\lambda + \varphi^{T}P\varphi)$, update $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) e(t)$, covariance $P(t) = [I - K\varphi^{T}]P(t-1)/\lambda$.
- **Certainty equivalence**: implement the controller as if $\hat{\theta} = \theta$; validity is asymptotic.
- **STR algorithm runs at every step**: measure → predict → error → RLS update → re-solve Diophantine → compute $u(t)$ → apply → loop.
- **Stability of STR** requires persistent excitation of the regressor and bounded disturbances.
- **Forgetting factor**: $\lambda = 1$ → no tracking of time variation; $\lambda < 1$ → tracks drift but amplifies noise; typical value $0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.99$.
- **Whiteness of $\omega(t)$** is the key stochastic assumption — the entire MVC derivation hinges on it.
- **Direct vs Indirect STR**: indirect STR (the one in this module) estimates plant parameters explicitly and re-designs the control law; direct STR parameterizes the controller directly and estimates controller parameters.
- **Real-world applications**: paper mills, distillation columns, CSTRs, adaptive autopilots, robotic manipulators.
- **Exam-ready formula trio**: ARMAX plant + Diophantine identity + RLS update — commit these three equations to memory verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unification of Direct Self Tuning Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unification of Direct Self Tuning Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Unification of Direct Self-Tuning Regulators (DSTR)** refers to a generalized theoretical framework that demonstrates how seemingly different adaptive control strategies — such as **Minimum Variance (MV)**, **Generalized Minimum Variance (GMV)**, **Linear Quadratic Gaussian (LQG) self-tuners**, and **Pole-Placement self-tuners** — can be derived as special cases of a **single underlying generalized cost function** with appropriately chosen weighting polynomials.

The unification is achieved by expressing a generalized performance index in the **z-domain (or δ-domain)**:

$$J(u, t) = E\left\{\left[P(z^{-1})\,y(t+k) - Q(z^{-1})\,w(t) + R(z^{-1})\,u(t)\right]^2\right\}$$

where $P(z^{-1})$, $Q(z^{-1})$, and $R(z^{-1})$ are **user-defined weighting polynomials** that shape the closed-loop behavior, and $w(t)$ is the **setpoint/reference signal**. By selecting different polynomials, the designer obtains different classical regulators *without* changing the underlying architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight):** The phrase **"Direct"** in DSTR means the controller parameters are estimated **directly in feedback/control law form** (e.g., the coefficients of $u(t)$) without explicitly identifying a process model. This is in contrast to **Indirect** schemes, where a process model is first estimated and then the controller is redesigned.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Engine" View

Imagine a **kitchen master-chef robot** that has only one cooking pot but contains three adjustable knobs: **Knob-P (heat distribution)**, **Knob-Q (spice intensity)**, and **Knob-R (oil/richness)**.

- Every recipe (biryani, pasta, dosa, soup) is cooked in the **same pot** (unified cost function).
- Changing the **combination of knob settings** yields entirely different dishes (different regulators).
- The robot's internal thermometer (system output) and gas knob (control input) are the only variables that matter.

In adaptive control, this pot is the **generalized quadratic cost**, the knobs are the **polynomial weights**, and the recipes are **MV, GMV, LQG, and Pole-Placement controllers**. A single, unified adaptive law produces them all.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The unification concept was prominently formalized by **D.W. Clarke** (Generalized Minimum Variance, 1975) and further extended in works by **Astrom & Wittenmark** and **Goodwin & Sin**, allowing KTU examiners to attribute the framework to a *family of contributors* rather than a single author.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Form | Engineering Meaning |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Sampling Period | $T_s$ | **0.1 s to 10 s** | Discretization interval |
| Adaptation Gain | $\lambda$ | $0.95 \le \lambda \le 1$ | Forgetting factor in RLS |
| Dead Time | $k$ | $1 \le k \le 5$ (integer) | Pure delay in samples |
| Output Weight | $P(z^{-1})$ | Polynomial in $z^{-1}$ | Reference tracking & pole shaping |
| Control Weight | $R(z^{-1})$ | Scalar $\rho$ often | Penalizes control effort |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Unified Cost Function

The unified cost function penalizes the deviation between a **filtered/processed output** $P(z^{-1})y(t+k)$ and a **reference/command trajectory** $Q(z^{-1})w(t)$, while simultaneously penalizing excessive control action through $R(z^{-1})u(t)$.

### 2.1.1 Why Three Polynomials?

1. **$P(z^{-1})$ — Output Filter / Closed-Loop Pole Shaper**  
   Selecting $P(z^{-1}) = 1$ forces the output to track the reference aggressively. Choosing $P(z^{-1}) = \text{desired closed-loop denominator}$ allows pole-placement behavior.

2. **$Q(z^{-1})$ — Reference/Setpoint Shaper**  
   Determines the *shape* of the trajectory the output should follow. $Q(z^{-1}) = 1$ gives step-reference tracking.

3. **$R(z^{-1})$ — Control Effort Penalizer**  
   A non-zero $R$ prevents actuator saturation and reduces variance in $u(t)$, at the cost of slower response.

### 2.1.2 The Control Law Derivation Logic

Starting from the controlled ARMA model:

$$A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = z^{-k} B(z^{-1})\,u(t) + C(z^{-1})\,e(t)$$

the $k$-step ahead optimal prediction $\hat{y}(t+k \mid t)$ is used. The **minimum** of $J$ with respect to $u(t)$ yields:

$$F(z^{-1})\,y(t) + G(z^{-1})\,y^*(t-k) = u(t) \cdot \text{(gain term)}$$

which, when recast in feedback form, gives the **direct self-tuning regulator**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in 90% of KTU numericals on this topic.**

| # | Name | Equation | Polynomial Choices | Resulting Regulator |
|---|------|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Unified Cost | $J = E\{[Py(t+k) - Qw + Ru]^2\}$ | Generic | Unified DSTR |
| 2 | **Minimum Variance (MV)** | $J_{MV} = E\{y^2(t+k)\}$ | $P=1,\ Q=0,\ R=0$ | MV Self-Tuner |
| 3 | **Generalized Minimum Variance (GMV)** | $J_{GMV} = E\{[Py - Qw]^2\}$ | $P=P_n/P_d,\ Q=1,\ R=0$ | GMV (Clarke) |
| 4 | **Linear Quadratic (LQ) Self-Tuner** | $J_{LQ} = E\{[y - w]^2 + \rho\, u^2\}$ | $P=1,\ Q=1,\ R=\rho$ | LQ Self-Tuner |
| 5 | **Pole-Placement DSTR** | $P = T(z^{-1})/A(z^{-1})$ | $P =$ desired closed-loop | Pole-Placement |
| 6 | **CARMA Model** | $A y(t) = z^{-k} B u(t) + C e(t)$ | — | Plant Representation |
| 7 | **Diophantine Identity** | $P C = E A + z^{-k} F$ | — | Predictor Foundation |
| 8 | **Adaptive Law (RLS)** | $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \varepsilon(t)$ | $K(t) = \frac{P(t-1)\phi(t)}{\lambda + \phi^T P \phi}$ | Parameter Update |
| 9 | **Control Law** | $u(t) = \frac{Q w(t) - F y(t) - G y^*(t-k)}{\cdot}$ | — | DSTR Output |
| 10 | **Stability Condition** | $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all closed-loop poles | — | BIBO Stability |

> **Note on notation:** Vertical bars in tables use $\vert$ for absolute value to preserve markdown rendering.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Unified Framework

| Engineering Domain | Application of Unified DSTR |
|--------------------|------------------------------|
| **Chemical Process Control** | pH neutralization, distillation columns (poles vary with feed composition) |
| **Robotics & CNC Machines** | Adaptive tracking where payload mass changes the closed-loop poles |
| **Aerospace (Autopilots)** | Reconfigurable flight control with damage/fault adaptation |
| **Automotive (Active Suspension)** | Real-time damping adjustment as road conditions change |
| **Power Electronics** | DC-DC converters with variable load (R changes ⇒ need RLS) |

The unification gives the engineer a **single tuning dashboard** for $P, Q, R$ to navigate between aggressive and conservative control without re-architecting the controller — saving design time and ensuring stable convergence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Unified Cost to Direct Control Law

### Step 1 — Define the Plant

Consider a **CARMA (Controlled Auto-Regressive Moving Average)** discrete-time model with integer delay $k$:

$$A(z^{-1})\,y(t) = z^{-k} B(z^{-1})\,u(t) + C(z^{-1})\,e(t)$$

where:
- $A(z^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_{n_a} z^{-n_a}$
- $B(z^{-1}) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \dots + b_{n_b} z^{-n_b}$
- $C(z^{-1}) = 1 + c_1 z^{-1} + \dots + c_{n_c} z^{-n_c}$
- $e(t)$ is **white noise** with $E\{e(t)\} = 0$, $E\{e^2(t)\} = \sigma^2$.

---

### Step 2 — Set Up the Generalized Cost

$$J(u, t) = E\left\{\left[P(z^{-1})\,y(t+k) - Q(z^{-1})\,w(t) + R(z^{-1})\,u(t)\right]^2 \,\Big|\, t\right\}$$

The conditional expectation is taken given the data available up to time $t$.

---

### Step 3 — Diophantine Equation (Predictor Kernel)

The **Diophantine identity** is introduced as the mathematical bridge between past and future:

$$P(z^{-1})\,C(z^{-1}) = E(z^{-1})\,A(z^{-1}) + z^{-k} F(z^{-1})$$

where:
- $E(z^{-1}) = e_0 + e_1 z^{-1} + \dots + e_{k-1} z^{-(k-1)}$ (degree $k-1$)
- $F(z^{-1}) = f_0 + f_1 z^{-1} + \dots + f_{n_f} z^{-n_f}$ (degree $\max(n_a-1, n_p + n_c - k)$)

---

### Step 4 — Compute the k-Step Ahead Prediction

Multiplying the CARMA model by $E(z^{-1}) z^{k}$ and rearranging:

$$\begin{aligned}
E(z^{-1}) A(z^{-1}) y(t) &= E(z^{-1}) z^{-k} B u(t) + E(z^{-1}) C e(t) \\
\left[P C - z^{-k} F\right] y(t) &= E z^{-k} B u(t) + E C e(t) \\
P C y(t) &= E z^{-k} B u(t) + z^{-k} F y(t) + E C e(t) \\
P\,y(t+k) &= E B u(t) + F y(t) + E e(t+k)
\end{aligned}$$

The $k$-step ahead prediction (since $E e(t+k) = 0$ for white noise with $k \ge 1$):

$$\hat{y}(t+k \mid t) = \frac{E(z^{-1}) B(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,u(t) + \frac{F(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,y(t)$$

---

### Step 5 — Minimize the Cost

The minimum of $J$ with respect to $u(t)$ is found by setting the derivative to zero. Since the *prediction error* is orthogonal to past data:

$$E\left\{\left[\text{future innovation}\right] \cdot u(t)\right\} = 0$$

This gives the **optimality condition**:

$$P(z^{-1})\,\hat{y}(t+k \mid t) - Q(z^{-1})\,w(t) + R(z^{-1})\,u(t) = 0$$

Substituting the prediction:

$$P \left[\frac{E B}{C} u(t) + \frac{F}{C} y(t)\right] - Q w(t) + R u(t) = 0$$

---

### Step 6 — Solve for $u(t)$ (The Control Law)

Multiply through by $C(z^{-1})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P E B\, u(t) + P F\, y(t) - Q C\, w(t) + R C\, u(t) &= 0 \\
\left[P E B + R C\right] u(t) &= Q C\, w(t) - P F\, y(t) \\
u(t) &= \frac{Q(z^{-1}) C(z^{-1})\,w(t) - P(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})\,y(t)}{P(z^{-1}) E(z^{-1}) B(z^{-1}) + R(z^{-1}) C(z^{-1})}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The DSTR Magic:** When **estimated recursively** (e.g., via RLS), the parameters of $u(t) = \theta^T \phi(t)$ are directly updated *without* explicitly identifying $A, B, C$. This is the *direct* adaptive law.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reducing to Specific Regulators

### 3.2.1 Reduction to **Minimum Variance (MV)**

Setting $P=1$, $Q=0$, $R=0$:

$$u(t) = -\frac{F(z^{-1})}{E(z^{-1}) B(z^{-1})}\,y(t)$$

This is the classical Astrom-Wittenmark MV regulator (1973).

### 3.2.2 Reduction to **Generalized Minimum Variance (GMV)**

Setting $P(z^{-1}) = 1 - \alpha z^{-1}$ (a first-order smoother), $Q = 1$, $R = 0$:

$$u(t) = \frac{C w(t) - F y(t)}{E B}$$

Clarke's GMV adds a tunable **smoothing factor** $\alpha$ to make MV robust.

### 3.2.3 Reduction to **Pole-Placement DSTR**

Setting $P(z^{-1}) = A_m(z^{-1}) / A(z^{-1})$ where $A_m$ is the **desired closed-loop denominator**:

$$u(t) = \frac{A_m C\,w(t) - A_m F\,y(t)}{A_m E B + R A C}$$

This places the closed-loop poles at the roots of $A_m$, **independent of plant zero locations**.

### 3.2.4 Reduction to **LQ / Predictive Self-Tuner**

Setting $P = 1$, $Q = 1$, $R = \rho$ (scalar):

$$u(t) = \frac{C\,w(t) - F\,y(t)}{E B + \rho C}$$

This is the **linear quadratic** self-tuner with control-weight $\rho$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Regulator Selection Guide

| Design Goal | Choose $P$ | Choose $Q$ | Choose $R$ | Result |
|-------------|------------|------------|------------|--------|
| Fastest output regulation (assume minimum phase) | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | **MV** |
| MV with smoother control | $1 - \alpha z^{-1}$ | $1$ | $0$ | **GMV** |
| Track changing setpoint with effort penalty | $1$ | $1$ | $\rho$ | **LQ** |
| Force specific closed-loop poles | $A_m / A$ | $1$ | $0$ | **Pole-Placement** |
| Long-horizon predictive control | $1$ | trajectory | $\rho$ | **GPC** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Unified DSTR Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference w of t] --> errSum{Junction}
    out[Output y of t] --> plant[Plant G process z]
    plant --> out
    out --> filtP[Filter P z inverse1]
    filtP --> errSum
    errSum --> cost[Generalized Cost J]
    uAct[Control u of t] --> filtR[Filter R z inverse1]
    filtR --> cost
    cost --> adaptLaw[Adaptive Law RLS]
    adaptLaw --> paramStore[Parameter Vector theta hat]
    paramStore --> ctrlLaw[Control Law Computation]
    ctrlLaw --> uAct
    uAct --> plant

    classDef blockStyle fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef signalStyle fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlStyle fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class filtP,filtR,cost blockStyle
    class ref,out,uAct signalStyle
    class adaptLaw,paramStore,ctrlLaw ctrlStyle
```

> **Reading the diagram:**  
> The **plant** output $y(t)$ is filtered by $P(z^{-1})$ and compared with the reference $w(t)$. The error, plus a control-weighted $R u(t)$, drives the **adaptive law** (RLS), which updates $\hat{\theta}$. The **control law** then produces $u(t)$, closing the loop. The same loop, with different $(P, Q, R)$ settings, gives MV, GMV, LQ, or pole-placement behavior.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: How the Three Weights Morph the Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    unified[Unified DSTR Cost J] --> choiceA{Choose P Q R}
    choiceA -->|P1 Q0 R0| mv[Minimum Variance MV]
    choiceA -->|P1 minus alpha z Q1 R0| gmv[Generalized Minimum Variance GMV]
    choiceA -->|P1 Q1 Rrho| lq[Linear Quadratic LQ Self Tuner]
    choiceA -->|P Am over A Q1 R0| pp[Pole Placement DSTR]
    choiceA -->|P1 Q trajectory Rrho| gpc[Generalized Predictive GPC]

    mv --> sharedDiophantine[Shared Diophantine Solver]
    gmv --> sharedDiophantine
    lq --> sharedDiophantine
    pp --> sharedDiophantine
    gpc --> sharedDiophantine

    sharedDiophantine --> controlOut[Common Control Law u of t]

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Adaptive Law Internals (RLS Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    measY[Measured y of t] --> regressor[Regress into phi of t]
    measU[Past u of t] --> regressor
    regressor --> innovation[Innovation eps of t equals y minus phi transpose theta hat]
    covMat[Covariance P of t] --> gain[Gain K of t equals P phi over lambda plus phi transpose P phi]
    regressor --> gain
    gain --> update[Update theta hat equals theta hat plus K times eps]
    update --> predict[Predict y hat of t plus k]
    predict --> ctrl[Compute u of t from Control Law]
    ctrl --> measU

```

> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The RLS update is **shared by all unified DSTR variants**. The examiner can swap the cost function $J$ without changing the *parameter adaptation* block — this is the **unification's central insight**.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Stability & Pole-Mapping Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    diophantine --> selection{Polynomial P Selection}

    selection -->|P equals 1| mvMap[Closed Loop Poles equals roots of C over B plus F]
    selection -->|P equals 1 minus alpha z| gmvMap[Closed Loop Poles placed via alpha]
    ppMap --> stabilityCheck{All poles inside Unit Circle}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Q:** Define the **Unified Cost Function** for a Direct Self-Tuning Regulator and explain how the choice of weighting polynomials $P(z^{-1})$, $Q(z^{-1})$, and $R(z^{-1})$ leads to different regulator structures.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The unified cost function is expressed as:

$$J(u,t) = E\left\{\left[P(z^{-1}) y(t+k) - Q(z^{-1}) w(t) + R(z^{-1}) u(t)\right]^2 \,\Big|\, t\right\}$$

- **$P(z^{-1})$** — Defines the *filter* on the output, influencing tracking and closed-loop pole locations.
- **$Q(z^{-1})$** — Shapes the *reference* trajectory; usually $Q=1$ for step references.
- **$R(z^{-1})$** — Penalizes *control effort*; larger $R$ yields slower but more conservative control.

By setting specific values:
- $P=1, Q=0, R=0$ → **Minimum Variance**
- $P=1-\alpha z^{-1}, Q=1, R=0$ → **Generalized Minimum Variance**
- $P=1, Q=1, R=\rho$ → **LQ Self-Tuner**
- $P = A_m/A, Q=1, R=0$ → **Pole-Placement DSTR**

*(1 mark for equation + 1 mark for polynomial roles + 1 mark for mapping to specific regulators)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** What is the difference between a **Direct** and an **Indirect** self-tuning regulator? Why is the Direct approach considered the "unifying" one?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct STR | Indirect STR |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Estimation Target | Controller parameters ($\theta$) | Plant model parameters ($A, B, C$) |
| Design Step | None — controller parameters are updated directly | Re-design controller from estimated model |
| Computation | Lower | Higher (involves Diophantine + redesign) |
| Tuning Surface | Single — works for $P, Q, R$ families | Multiple — depends on design method |

The **Direct** approach is unifying because the *same* adaptive update law — $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \varepsilon(t)$ — produces MV, GMV, LQ, and Pole-Placement controllers merely by changing the cost function. This is **the unification** of DSTRs. *(3 marks: 1 for table comparison, 1 for explanation, 1 for unification insight)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Slightly Modified]`

**Q:** Consider a discrete-time plant governed by the CARMA model:
$$y(t) - 0.5 y(t-1) = 0.2 u(t-2) + e(t)$$
where $e(t)$ is white noise with zero mean and unit variance, and the reference is $w(t) = 1$ (constant setpoint).

**(a)** **(7 marks)** Derive the **Unified Direct Self-Tuning Regulator** control law for the above plant. Choose $P(z^{-1}) = 1$, $Q(z^{-1}) = 1$, $R(z^{-1}) = 0.1$ (constant). Show the Diophantine equation and identify $E(z^{-1})$ and $F(z^{-1})$.

**(b)** **(7 marks)** Show that with $R=0$ the regulator reduces to the **Generalized Minimum Variance (GMV)** form, and discuss the role of the smoothing constant $\alpha$ in handling non-minimum phase plants.

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify $A, B, C, k$:**  
$A(z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.5 z^{-1}$  
$B(z^{-1}) = 0.2$  
$C(z^{-1}) = 1$  
$k = 2$

**Step 2 — Diophantine equation:**  
$P C = E A + z^{-k} F$ with $P = 1$:

$$1 \cdot 1 = E(z^{-1})(1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) + z^{-2} F(z^{-1})$$

Choose $E(z^{-1}) = e_0$ (degree $k-1 = 1$):

$$1 = e_0(1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) + z^{-2} f_0$$

Matching powers: $e_0 = 1$, then $z^{-1}$: $-0.5 e_0 + 0 = 0 \Rightarrow$ residual at $z^{-1}$ is $-0.5$, so we need $z^{-2} f_0$ to clear it? Actually matching $z^{-1}$:

Coefficient of $z^{-1}$: $-0.5 e_0 = -0.5$.  
But the LHS has no $z^{-1}$ term, so we set $F(z^{-1})$ to absorb. Take $F(z^{-1}) = -0.5$ (degree 0):

$$1 = 1 \cdot (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) + z^{-2}(-0.5) = 1 - 0.5 z^{-1} - 0.5 z^{-2}$$

But this leaves a $z^{-2}$ term on RHS. So we need $E$ of degree 2:

$$E = 1 + e_1 z^{-1} + e_2 z^{-2}$$ 

Trying $E = 1$ and $F = -0.5$: gives $1 - 0.5 z^{-1} - 0.5 z^{-2} \ne 1$. So the correct choice: **$E(z^{-1}) = 1$, $F(z^{-1}) = 0.5 z^{-1}$**? Let us re-derive:

$$1 = 1 \cdot (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) + z^{-2}(f_0 + f_1 z^{-1})$$
$$1 = 1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + f_0 z^{-2} + f_1 z^{-3}$$

To match LHS $= 1$:  
- $z^0$: $1 = 1$ ✓  
- $z^{-1}$: $0 = -0.5$ ✗

So $E$ must contain a $z^{-1}$ term to cancel $-0.5 z^{-1}$:

Let $E = 1 + e_1 z^{-1}$, $F = f_0$:

$$1 = (1 + e_1 z^{-1})(1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) + z^{-2} f_0$$
$$= 1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + e_1 z^{-1} - 0.5 e_1 z^{-2} + f_0 z^{-2}$$
$$= 1 + (e_1 - 0.5) z^{-1} + (f_0 - 0.5 e_1) z^{-2}$$

Matching:
- $z^{-1}$: $0 = e_1 - 0.5 \Rightarrow e_1 = 0.5$
- $z^{-2}$: $0 = f_0 - 0.5(0.5) \Rightarrow f_0 = 0.25$

**Thus:** $E(z^{-1}) = 1 + 0.5 z^{-1}$, $F(z^{-1}) = 0.25$ *(degree $k-1=1$ for $E$, degree $n_a - 1 = 0$ for $F$)*.

**Step 3 — Control law:**  
Substitute $P=1$, $Q=1$, $R=0.1$ into:

$$u(t) = \frac{Q C w(t) - P F y(t)}{P E B + R C}$$

$$u(t) = \frac{(1)(1)(1) - (1)(0.25) y(t)}{(1)(1+0.5 z^{-1})(0.2) + (0.1)(1)}$$

$$u(t) = \frac{1 - 0.25 y(t)}{0.2 + 0.1 z^{-1} + 0.1} = \frac{1 - 0.25 y(t)}{0.3 + 0.1 z^{-1}}$$

Multiplying out:
$$(0.3 + 0.1 z^{-1}) u(t) = 1 - 0.25 y(t)$$
$$0.3 u(t) + 0.1 u(t-1) = 1 - 0.25 y(t)$$
$$u(t) = \frac{1 - 0.25 y(t) - 0.1 u(t-1)}{0.3}$$

This is the **unified DSTR control law** for the given plant. *(7 marks: 2 for Diophantine, 2 for substitution, 2 for final control law, 1 for concluding statement)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b):**

**Setting $R = 0$** in the unified cost:

$$J_{GMV} = E\left\{\left[P(z^{-1}) y(t+k) - Q(z^{-1}) w(t)\right]^2\right\}$$

The control law becomes:

$$u(t) = \frac{Q C w(t) - P F y(t)}{P E B}$$

If we further choose $P(z^{-1}) = 1 - \alpha z^{-1}$ (a first-order **smoothing filter**), this is **Clarke's GMV law**.

**Role of Smoothing Constant $\alpha$:**

1. **For minimum-phase plants** ($B(z^{-1})$ has stable zeros), $\alpha = 0$ recovers the MV regulator.
2. **For non-minimum phase plants**, MV fails because it places poles at the **inverse of plant zeros** (unstable). By increasing $\alpha$ ($0 < \alpha < 1$), $P(z^{-1})$ is designed to **cancel the unstable zero** by the identity $P(z^{-1}) = 1 - \alpha z^{-1}$ where $\alpha$ corresponds to the unstable zero's location.
3. **Smoothing**: $\alpha$ also reduces **control variance** — large $\alpha$ ⇒ smoother $u(t)$ ⇒ less actuator wear.
4. **Trade-off**: Larger $\alpha$ gives slower output response (delayed tracking), so the engineer must balance speed vs. stability robustness.

**Concluding Statement:** Hence, GMV unifies MV (with $\alpha = 0$) and provides a tunable parameter to handle a wider class of plants — making it a generalization of MV within the **unified DSTR** framework. *(7 marks: 2 for substitution, 2 for $\alpha$ role, 1 for non-minimum phase handling, 1 for trade-offs, 1 for conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**Q:** (a) **(7 marks)** Explain, with a detailed block diagram, the **architecture of a Direct Self-Tuning Regulator**. Show how the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** algorithm updates the controller parameters in real time.

**(b)** **(7 marks)** A pole-placement DSTR is to be designed for a plant $G(z) = \frac{0.1 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$ with desired closed-loop poles at $z = 0.5$ (double pole). Derive the control law using the **unified cost** framework with $P = A_m/A$, $Q = 1$, $R = 0$.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a):**

**Architecture of DSTR (Block Diagram in words):**

1. **Plant $G(z)$**: Produces output $y(t)$ from input $u(t-1)$ plus disturbance $v(t)$.
2. **Reference Generator**: Provides $w(t)$.
3. **Regressor Formulation**: $\phi(t) = [-y(t-1), \dots, -y(t-n_a), u(t-1-k), \dots, u(t-n_b-k)]^T$
4. **Parameter Estimator (RLS)**:  
   - Prediction error: $\varepsilon(t) = y(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t-1) \phi(t)$  
   - Gain: $K(t) = \dfrac{P(t-1) \phi(t)}{\lambda + \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)}$  
   - Update: $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \varepsilon(t)$  
   - Covariance: $P(t) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left[P(t-1) - K(t) \phi^T(t) P(t-1)\right]$
5. **Control Law Calculator**: Computes $u(t)$ using the updated $\hat{\theta}$.

**Why Real-Time?** The estimator runs every sampling instant $T_s$. As $\hat{\theta}$ converges, $u(t)$ adapts to track the optimum — making the system *self-tuning*.

**RLS in detail:**  
The forgetting factor $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ gives more weight to recent data; $\lambda < 1$ is essential for **slowly time-varying plants**. The convergence of $\hat{\theta} \to \theta^*$ is guaranteed if the regressor $\phi(t)$ is *persistently exciting* (PE condition). *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for RLS equations, 2 for convergence note)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify $A, B, C, k$:**  
$A(z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.8 z^{-1}$  
$B(z^{-1}) = 0.1$  
$C(z^{-1}) = 1$ (assumed)  
$k = 1$ (single-sample delay)

**Step 2 — Desired closed-loop pole polynomial:**  
Desired poles at $z = 0.5$ (double) ⇒ $A_m(z^{-1}) = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2 = 1 - z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2}$

**Step 3 — Choose $P = A_m / A$:**

$$P(z^{-1}) = \frac{1 - z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Diophantine Equation:** $P C = E A + z^{-k} F$ with $k = 1$:

$$\frac{(1 - z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2})}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}} = E(z^{-1}) + z^{-1} F(z^{-1})$$

Multiply both sides by $(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})$:

$$1 - z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2} = (1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) E(z^{-1}) + z^{-1}(1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) F(z^{-1})$$

Let $E = e_0$ (constant) and $F = f_0 + f_1 z^{-1}$ (degree 1):

$$1 - z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2} = e_0 - 0.8 e_0 z^{-1} + f_0 z^{-1} - 0.8 f_0 z^{-2} + f_1 z^{-2}$$

Matching powers:
- $z^0$: $1 = e_0 \Rightarrow e_0 = 1$
- $z^{-1}$: $-1 = -0.8 + f_0 \Rightarrow f_0 = -0.2$
- $z^{-2}$: $0.25 = -0.8(-0.2) + f_1 = 0.16 + f_1 \Rightarrow f_1 = 0.09$

**Step 5 — Control Law with $Q=1$, $R=0$:**  
$$u(t) = \frac{Q C w(t) - P F y(t)}{P E B} = \frac{w(t) - P F y(t)}{P \cdot 1 \cdot B}$$

Compute $P F = \frac{A_m}{A} \cdot F = \frac{(1 - z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2})(f_0 + f_1 z^{-1})}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$

Substituting $f_0 = -0.2$, $f_1 = 0.09$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P F &= \frac{(1 - z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2})(-0.2 + 0.09 z^{-1})}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}} \\
&= \frac{-0.2 + 0.09 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-1} - 0.09 z^{-2} - 0.05 z^{-2} + 0.0225 z^{-3}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}} \\
&= \frac{-0.2 + 0.29 z^{-1} - 0.14 z^{-2} + 0.0225 z^{-3}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final control law:**

$$u(t) = \frac{w(t) - P F\, y(t)}{0.1 \, P} = 10\,\frac{w(t) - P F\, y(t)}{P}$$

Substituting $P = A_m/A$:

$$u(t) = 10\,\frac{A(z^{-1})}{A_m(z^{-1})}\left[w(t) - P F\, y(t)\right]$$

This is the **pole-placement DSTR control law** that forces closed-loop poles to $z = 0.5$ (double). *(7 marks: 2 for plant identification, 1 for $A_m$, 2 for Diophantine, 2 for control law)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the delay $k$** in the Diophantine: many students set $z^{-k} = 1$. **Always retain $z^{-k}$** in the identity.
> 2. **Mixing $P, Q, R$ definitions**: KTU uses $P$ as output filter (not the same as the $P$ matrix in RLS covariance). Use distinct symbols.
> 3. **Skipping the final scalar $u(t)$ simplification**: writing only the polynomial ratio loses 1–2 marks. Always convert to a **time-domain difference equation** for full credit.
> 4. **Assuming plant is minimum phase** for MV: this is a *fatal* assumption. Mention the non-minimum phase caveat even if the question doesn't ask.
> 5. **Not showing PE condition**: in RLS derivations, omitting the **persistent excitation** condition forfeits 1 mark for stability discussion.
> 6. **Units mismatch**: when $P$ has higher degree than $A$, the ratio is improper — you must perform **polynomial long division** first to separate the causal part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Quick-Reference Summary (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [x] **Unified DSTR Cost** = $E\{[P y(t+k) - Q w + R u]^2\}$ — single framework, three knobs.
- [x] **Direct STR** estimates $\theta$ (control law parameters) directly; **Indirect** estimates $A, B, C$ first.
- [x] **MV Regulator**: $P=1, Q=0, R=0$ — fastest, but fails for **non-minimum phase** plants.
- [x] **GMV Regulator**: $P=1-\alpha z^{-1}, Q=1, R=0$ — $\alpha$ smooths control and handles non-minimum phase.
- [x] **LQ Self-Tuner**: $P=1, Q=1, R=\rho$ — penalizes control effort, gives robust tracking.
- [x] **Pole-Placement DSTR**: $P = A_m/A, Q=1, R=0$ — exact closed-loop pole assignment, **independent of plant zeros**.
- [x] **Diophantine Identity**: $P C = E A + z^{-k} F$ is the **core math engine** for prediction.
- [x] **Control Law**: $u(t) = \dfrac{Q C w - P F y}{P E B + R C}$ — universally applicable.
- [x] **RLS Update**: $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \varepsilon(t)$, with forgetting factor $\lambda \in (0.95, 1)$.
- [x] **Stability**: All closed-loop poles must satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$.
- [x] **Persistent Excitation (PE)**: $\phi(t)$ must be PE for $\hat{\theta} \to \theta^*$.
- [x] **Block Diagram**: Plant → Output filter $P$ → Compare with $Q w$ → Adaptive law → Control $u(t)$.
- [x] **KTU Emphasis**: Most Part B questions (14 marks) ask for **Diophantine solution + control law derivation**; practice these 3–4 times before the exam.
- [x] **Examiner's Hot Topics**: (i) Reduction of unified cost to MV/GMV/LQ (ii) Pole-placement DSTR (iii) RLS convergence (iv) Non-minimum phase handling via GMV.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Quadratic STR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Quadratic STR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Quadratic Self-Tuning Regulator (LQ-STR)** is an indirect adaptive control strategy that combines **online parameter estimation** (typically using Recursive Least Squares) with **Linear Quadratic (LQ) optimal control** design. At every sampling instant, the unknown plant parameters are identified, the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)** is solved on-line to compute the optimal state feedback gain, and this gain is used to generate the control signal that minimizes a predefined quadratic performance index.

Mathematically, the LQ-STR is defined as a recursive three-stage procedure operating on a discrete-time linear plant of the form:

$$x(k+1) = A \, x(k) + B \, u(k)$$

where the matrices $A$ and $B$ are unknown but estimated at each step, and the control law

$$u(k) = -L(k) \, \hat{x}(k)$$

is computed by minimizing the infinite-horizon cost functional

$$J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left[ x^{T}(k) Q x(k) + u^{T}(k) R u(k) \right]$$

with $Q = Q^{T} \succeq 0$ and $R = R^{T} \succ 0$.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Self-Adjusting Cruise Control of a Car**

Imagine you are driving a car whose exact mass, air-drag coefficient, and engine response you do *not* know. Your goal is to keep the speed as close as possible to a target while also being gentle on the accelerator (to save fuel and ensure passenger comfort). 

1. **Identification Stage**: You keep watching how the car responds when you press the pedal. From this observation, you keep *updating* your mental model of the car — "Ah, the car is heavier than I thought!" This is the **RLS estimator**.

2. **Optimization Stage**: Once you have an updated model, you sit down (in your head) and ask: *"Given what I now know about the car, what is the most fuel-efficient pedal-pressing strategy?"* This involves solving a small optimization problem — this is the **LQ step (solving the Riccati equation)**.

3. **Actuation Stage**: You apply the optimal pedal position. Then the cycle repeats.

The "self-tuning" nature comes from the fact that you never stop learning about the car, and the "Linear Quadratic" part comes from the cost function being a quadratic sum of (speed-error)² and (pedal-effort)². This analogy makes LQ-STR very intuitive for first-time learners.

---

### Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Type |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $Q$ | State weighting matrix | $n \times n$ symmetric positive semi-definite |
| $R$ | Input weighting matrix | $m \times m$ symmetric positive definite |
| $P$ | Riccati solution matrix | $n \times n$ symmetric positive definite |
| $L$ | Optimal feedback gain | $m \times n$ |
| $\theta$ | Parameter vector | $n_\theta \times 1$ |
| $\lambda$ | Forgetting factor (RLS) | scalar, typically $0.95 \le \lambda \le 1$ |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU-favoured default is $Q = I_n$ and $R = \rho I_m$ where $\rho > 0$ is a tuning knob — increasing $\rho$ makes the controller "lazier" (uses less control effort at the cost of slower response).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Architecture of LQ-STR

The LQ-STR can be cleanly decomposed into three sequential stages, repeated at every sampling instant $k$:

- **Stage 1 — Parameter Estimation (RLS / RIV / ELS)**
  - *Why*: The true plant matrices $A, B$ are unknown and possibly time-varying. We must estimate them from input-output data $\{u(j), y(j)\}$ for $j \le k$.
  - *How*: Collect the regressor vector $\varphi(k)$ and the output $y(k)$, then apply the Recursive Least Squares update equations to refresh $\hat{\theta}(k)$.

- **Stage 2 — Regulator Design (Solve the ARE)**
  - *Why*: With the current estimate $\hat{A}(k), \hat{B}(k)$, we want the gain that *would* be optimal if these estimates were exact.
  - *How*: Solve the Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE) to get $\hat{P}(k)$, then compute $\hat{L}(k) = \left[R + \hat{B}^{T} \hat{P}(k) \hat{B}\right]^{-1} \hat{B}^{T}(k) \hat{P}(k) \hat{A}(k)$.

- **Stage 3 — Control Law Application**
  - *Why*: To physically steer the plant.
  - *How*: Apply $u(k) = -\hat{L}(k) \, x(k)$. If states are not directly measurable, replace $x(k)$ by an observer estimate $\hat{x}(k)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Certainty Equivalence Principle** — The LQ-STR design is grounded on the *certainty equivalence* concept: the controller behaves *as if* the estimated parameters were the true parameters. This is the central philosophical underpinning of the entire class of indirect self-tuning regulators.

---

### 2.2 Underlying Assumptions for LQ-STR to Work

1. The plant is **linear and time-invariant** (or slowly time-varying) of known order $n$ and input dimension $m$.
2. The pair $(\hat{A}, \hat{B})$ is **stabilizable** at every $k$ (this is the minimum-phase-like condition for LQ design).
3. The pair $(\hat{A}, Q^{1/2})$ is **detectable** (so the LQ cost reflects actual state behaviour).
4. The persistent excitation condition on the input signal holds (so RLS converges).
5. Sampling is performed at a sufficiently fast rate relative to the plant dynamics.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ | Nominal plant model (state-space) |
| 2 | $J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left[x^{T} Q x + u^{T} R u\right]$ | Infinite-horizon LQ cost |
| 3 | $A^{T} P A - P - A^{T} P B (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A + Q = 0$ | **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)** |
| 4 | $L = (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A$ | **Optimal feedback gain** |
| 5 | $u(k) = -L \, x(k)$ | Optimal state feedback law |
| 6 | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k) \left[ y(k) - \varphi^{T}(k) \hat{\theta}(k-1) \right]$ | RLS parameter update |
| 7 | $K(k) = \dfrac{P(k-1) \varphi(k)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(k) P(k-1) \varphi(k)}$ | RLS gain vector |
| 8 | $P(k) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \left[ I - K(k) \varphi^{T}(k) \right] P(k-1)$ | RLS covariance update |
| 9 | $0 < \lambda \le 1$ | Forgetting factor (handles time-variation) |
| 10 | $y(k) = \varphi^{T}(k) \theta$ | Regressor model for ARX / ARMAX structure |
| 11 | $\hat{x}(k+1) = \hat{A} \hat{x}(k) + \hat{B} u(k) + K_o \left[y(k) - C \hat{x}(k)\right]$ | State observer (if $x$ not measurable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the DARE** and the **gain formula** by heart — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this topic. Substitute the *estimated* $\hat{A}$ and $\hat{B}$ inside the DARE to obtain $\hat{P}$, then compute $\hat{L}$ from the gain formula.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

LQ-STR finds strong application in:

- **Aerospace**: Aircraft autopilot design where aerodynamic coefficients shift with altitude and Mach number.
- **Process Control**: Chemical reactors with time-varying reaction kinetics and catalyst deactivation.
- **Robotics**: Manipulators carrying varying payloads, where inertia and friction terms drift.
- **Automotive**: Active suspension and engine idle-speed control.
- **Power Systems**: Turbine-governor tuning with drifting load characteristics.

The principal engineering advantage is that the engineer need not perform an explicit, expensive system identification experiment — the controller *learns* while it *controls*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal LQ Gain

**Step 1 — Set up the problem.**

Given the deterministic discrete-time LTI system

$$x(k+1) = A \, x(k) + B \, u(k), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

and the infinite-horizon quadratic cost

$$J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left[ x^{T}(k) Q x(k) + u^{T}(k) R u(k) \right]$$

we seek the sequence $\{u^{*}(k)\}_{k=0}^{\infty}$ that minimizes $J$.

**Step 2 — Introduce the cost-to-go function.**

Define the optimal value function as

$$V(x(k)) = \min_{u(k), u(k+1), \dots} \sum_{j=k}^{\infty} \left[ x^{T}(j) Q x(j) + u^{T}(j) R u(j) \right]$$

By the principle of optimality (Dynamic Programming), this satisfies the **Bellman equation**

$$V(x(k)) = \min_{u(k)} \left\{ x^{T}(k) Q x(k) + u^{T}(k) R u(k) + V(x(k+1)) \right\}$$

**Step 3 — Postulate a quadratic value function.**

Assume the value function takes the form

$$V(x(k)) = x^{T}(k) P x(k)$$

for some symmetric positive-definite matrix $P$. (This ansatz is the cornerstone of the LQ theory; the assumption is later justified by the existence and uniqueness of $P$.)

**Step 4 — Substitute the ansatz into the Bellman equation.**

$$x^{T}(k) P x(k) = \min_{u(k)} \left\{ x^{T}(k) Q x(k) + u^{T}(k) R u(k) + x^{T}(k+1) P x(k+1) \right\}$$

Replace $x(k+1)$ with $A x(k) + B u(k)$:

$$x^{T}(k) P x(k) = \min_{u(k)} \left\{ x^{T}(k) Q x(k) + u^{T}(k) R u(k) + (A x(k) + B u(k))^{T} P (A x(k) + B u(k)) \right\}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the quadratic in $u(k)$.**

Let $f(u) = x^{T} Q x + u^{T} R u + (A x + B u)^{T} P (A x + B u)$. Expanding the third term:

$$(A x + B u)^{T} P (A x + B u) = x^{T} A^{T} P A x + 2 x^{T} A^{T} P B u + u^{T} B^{T} P B u$$

Therefore,

$$f(u) = x^{T}(Q + A^{T} P A) x + 2 x^{T} A^{T} P B u + u^{T}(R + B^{T} P B) u$$

**Step 6 — Minimize with respect to $u(k)$.**

Take $\frac{\partial f}{\partial u} = 0$ (first-order optimality condition for unconstrained quadratic in $u$):

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial u} = 2 (R + B^{T} P B) u + 2 B^{T} P A x = 0$$

Solving for the optimal $u$:

$$u^{*}(k) = -\left[ (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A \right] x(k)$$

Hence the optimal gain is

$$L = (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A$$

and the control law is $u(k) = -L \, x(k)$.

**Step 7 — Derive the Riccati equation.**

Substitute the optimal $u^{*}(k)$ back into the Bellman equation. After expansion and rearrangement (detailed algebra shown below):

$$x^{T} P x = x^{T} Q x + x^{T} A^{T} P A x - x^{T} A^{T} P B (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A x$$

This must hold for every $x$, so the matrix coefficients must match:

$$P = Q + A^{T} P A - A^{T} P B (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A$$

Rearranging into the canonical "Riccati = 0" form:

$$A^{T} P A - P - A^{T} P B (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A + Q = 0$$

This is the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)**. The unique symmetric positive-definite solution $P$ exists whenever $(A, B)$ is stabilizable and $(A, Q^{1/2})$ is detectable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all this matters for LQ-STR** — In a self-tuning regulator, we do *not* know $A$ and $B$ exactly. So we **substitute the on-line estimates $\hat{A}(k)$ and $\hat{B}(k)$** into the DARE and the gain formula, and *re-solve* the DARE at every step. The recursive identifier and the Riccati solver together constitute the "self-tuning" engine.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the RLS Update Equations

**Step 1 — Set up the linear regression model.**

Assume the SISO plant can be represented (after adequate model structure selection) as

$$y(k) = \varphi^{T}(k) \, \theta + e(k)$$

where $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n_\theta}$ is the unknown parameter vector, $\varphi(k)$ is the regressor of past inputs/outputs, and $e(k)$ is the equation-error.

**Step 2 — Define the exponentially-weighted least-squares cost.**

$$\mathcal{J}(\theta, k) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-j} \left[ y(j) - \varphi^{T}(j) \theta \right]^{2}$$

where $0 < \lambda \le 1$ is the forgetting factor.

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial \theta} = -2 \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-j} \varphi(j) \left[ y(j) - \varphi^{T}(j) \theta \right] = 0$$

Defining $P^{-1}(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-j} \varphi(j) \varphi^{T}(j)$, the offline solution is

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-j} \varphi(j) \varphi^{T}(j) \right]^{-1} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-j} \varphi(j) y(j) = P(k) \, \Phi(k)$$

**Step 4 — Convert offline to recursive form.**

Using the matrix inversion lemma on the covariance update yields:

$$P(k) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ P(k-1) - \frac{P(k-1) \varphi(k) \varphi^{T}(k) P(k-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(k) P(k-1) \varphi(k)} \right]$$

Defining the gain vector

$$K(k) = \frac{P(k-1) \varphi(k)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(k) P(k-1) \varphi(k)}$$

the parameter update becomes

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k) \left[ y(k) - \varphi^{T}(k) \hat{\theta}(k-1) \right]$$

These three coupled equations are the **RLS algorithm**.

---

### 3.3 Combining the Two: The LQ-STR Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Complete LQ-STR Recursion** (pseudo-procedure, *not* Python code)
>
> **Initialize:** $\hat{\theta}(0) = \theta_0$, $P(0) = \alpha I$ for large $\alpha > 0$, $u(-1) = 0$, $y(-1) = 0$. Choose $Q \succeq 0$, $R \succ 0$, $\lambda \in (0, 1]$.
>
> **At each sampling instant $k$ do:**
> 1. Read new measurement $y(k)$.
> 2. Form regressor $\varphi(k)$ from $\{u(j), y(j)\}$ for the past $n$ steps.
> 3. **RLS Step:** Update $\hat{\theta}(k)$ using the three RLS equations (formulas 6, 7, 8 above).
> 4. **Reconstruct matrices:** From $\hat{\theta}(k)$, build $\hat{A}(k)$ and $\hat{B}(k)$.
> 5. **Solve DARE:** Find $\hat{P}(k)$ satisfying the DARE with $\hat{A}(k), \hat{B}(k)$.
> 6. **Compute gain:** $\hat{L}(k) = (R + \hat{B}^{T} \hat{P} \hat{B})^{-1} \hat{B}^{T} \hat{P} \hat{A}$.
> 7. **Apply control:** $u(k) = -\hat{L}(k) \, x(k)$ (or with observer if $x$ is unmeasured).
> 8. **Wait** for next sample; return to step 1.

This eight-step loop is the **Linear Quadratic Self-Tuning Regulator** in its full operational form.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the LQ-STR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ref[k Reference Input] --> sumA((Sum Junction))
    plant[PLANT Process] --> meas[y(k) Measured Output]
    meas --> regressor[Regressor Builder phi k]
    regressor --> rls[RLS Estimator]
    rls --> matrices[Estimated Matrices Ahat Bhat]
    matrices --> dare[DARE Solver Riccati]
    dare --> pmat[Riccati Solution Phat]
    pmat --> gain[Gain Calculator Lhat = R + Bhat T Phat Bhat inv Bhat T Phat Ahat]
    gain --> ctrl[Controller u k = minus Lhat x k]
    ctrl --> sumA
    sumA --> plant
    ctrl --> plant
    plant --> obs[State Observer if x not measurable]
    obs --> ctrl
```

### 4.2 Information-Flow Diagram (Sequential Stages)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Parameter Estimation RLS] -->|theta hat k| S2[Stage 2 Design Solve DARE]
    S2 -->|L hat k| S3[Stage 3 Control Law u k]
    S3 -->|u k| Plant[Plant]
    Plant -->|y k| S1
    S1 -->|theta hat k| S4[State Observer if needed]
    S4 -->|x hat k| S3
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ID_LOOP["Identification Loop"]
        RLS[RLS Algorithm] --> MAT[Plant Model Estimates Ahat Bhat]
    end
    subgraph CTRL_LOOP["Control Loop"]
        DARE[DARE Solver] --> GAIN[Optimal Gain Lhat]
        GAIN --> APPLY[Control Signal u k]
    end
    MAT --> DARE
    APPLY --> SUM((Sum))
    REFr[Reference r k] --> SUM
    SUM --> PLANT[Plant G z]
    PLANT --> YK[Output y k]
    YK --> RLS
    YK --> OBSV[Observer xhat k]
    OBSV --> APPLY
    PLANT -.Feedback.-> YK
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram** — The **outer (slower) loop** is the identification path: from $y(k)$ through the RLS to fresh $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$ and then through the DARE to a new $\hat{L}$. The **inner (faster) loop** is the closed control loop using the most recent $\hat{L}$ to drive the plant. In KTU answers, always label both loops explicitly — examiners reward clarity of architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**  
Define a Linear Quadratic Self-Tuning Regulator. State the certainty equivalence principle upon which it is based.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
A **Linear Quadratic Self-Tuning Regulator (LQ-STR)** is an indirect adaptive control scheme in which the parameters of a linear plant are estimated on-line (typically using Recursive Least Squares), and these parameter estimates are used at every sampling instant to compute the optimal state-feedback gain that minimizes an infinite-horizon quadratic cost function  
$J = \sum \left[ x^{T} Q x + u^{T} R u \right]$ by solving the Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation. **(2 marks)**  
It is based on the **certainty equivalence principle**, which states that the controller is designed as if the current parameter estimates were the true values of the plant parameters. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
Write the Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation and the corresponding optimal state-feedback gain expression for a discrete LQ problem.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)** is:

$$A^{T} P A - P - A^{T} P B \left(R + B^{T} P B\right)^{-1} B^{T} P A + Q = 0 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

The corresponding optimal state-feedback gain is:

$$L = \left(R + B^{T} P B\right)^{-1} B^{T} P A \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the optimal state-feedback law for the infinite-horizon discrete-time LQ problem with the system $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ and cost $J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left[ x^{T}(k) Q x(k) + u^{T}(k) R u(k) \right]$. Clearly state the Riccati equation that must be satisfied. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a plant with $\hat{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $\hat{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, design the LQ gain with $Q = I_2$ and $R = 1$. Compute the closed-loop eigenvalues. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Optimal LQ Law Derivation** *(7 marks)*

1. **Bellman equation** (1 mark):
$$V(x(k)) = \min_{u(k)} \left\{ x^{T}(k) Q x(k) + u^{T}(k) R u(k) + V(x(k+1)) \right\}$$

2. **Quadratic ansatz** (1 mark): $V(x(k)) = x^{T}(k) P x(k)$.

3. **Substitute plant** (1 mark): $V(x(k+1)) = (A x + B u)^{T} P (A x + B u)$.

4. **First-order condition** (1 mark): Differentiating w.r.t. $u$ and setting to zero:
$$2(R + B^{T} P B) u + 2 B^{T} P A x = 0$$

5. **Optimal gain** (1 mark):  
$$L = (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A, \quad u^{*}(k) = -L x(k)$$

6. **Riccati equation** (2 marks): Substituting back:
$$A^{T} P A - P - A^{T} P B (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A + Q = 0$$

---

**(b) Numerical LQ Design** *(7 marks)*

Given $\hat{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\hat{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $Q = I_2$, $R = 1$.

1. **Posit DARE solution** (2 marks): Let $P = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$. Compute the matrix product $A^{T} P A$:

$$A^{T} P A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{11} + p_{12} & p_{12} + p_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{11} + p_{12} \\ p_{11} + p_{12} & p_{11} + 2 p_{12} + p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

2. **Compute the correction term** (1 mark): First, $B^{T} P B = p_{22}$, so $R + B^{T} P B = 1 + p_{22}$. Next, $B^{T} P A = \begin{bmatrix} p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$. Then the correction term is

$$\frac{A^{T} P B (B^{T} P A)}{1 + p_{22}} = \frac{1}{1 + p_{22}} \begin{bmatrix} p_{12} (p_{12}) & p_{12} (p_{22}) \\ p_{22} (p_{12}) & p_{22}^{2} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{1 + p_{22}} \begin{bmatrix} p_{12}^{2} & p_{12} p_{22} \\ p_{12} p_{22} & p_{22}^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

3. **Assemble DARE** (2 marks): The equation $A^{T} P A - P + Q = $ correction term becomes elementwise:

   - $(1,1)$: $p_{11} - p_{11} + 1 = \frac{p_{12}^{2}}{1 + p_{22}}$
   - $(1,2)$: $p_{11} + p_{12} - p_{12} = \frac{p_{12} p_{22}}{1 + p_{22}}$
   - $(2,2)$: $p_{11} + 2 p_{12} + p_{22} - p_{22} + 1 = \frac{p_{22}^{2}}{1 + p_{22}}$

   Simplifying:
   - From $(1,1)$: $1 = \frac{p_{12}^{2}}{1 + p_{22}} \Rightarrow p_{12}^{2} = 1 + p_{22}$
   - From $(1,2)$: $p_{11} = \frac{p_{12} p_{22}}{1 + p_{22}}$
   - From $(2,2)$: $p_{11} + 2 p_{12} + 1 = \frac{p_{22}^{2}}{1 + p_{22}}$
