4. **Solve** (1 mark): From $(1,1)$ and using $p_{12} > 0$ (stabilizing solution), $p_{12} = \sqrt{1 + p_{22}}$. Iterating yields $P = \begin{bmatrix} 2.414 & 1.414 \\ 1.414 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$ (a standard textbook solution for this canonical example).

5. **Compute the gain** (1 mark):

$$L = (1 + p_{22})^{-1} B^{T} P A = (1 + 1)^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 1.414 & 2.414 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 1.207 \end{bmatrix}$$

6. **Closed-loop eigenvalues** (1 mark — partial): The closed-loop matrix is $\hat{A} - \hat{B} L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -0.707 & -0.207 \end{bmatrix}$. Its characteristic polynomial is $\lambda^{2} - 0.793 \lambda + 0.086 \approx 0$, giving eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2} \approx 0.46 \pm j 0.16$, which lie inside the unit circle — confirming stability.

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`** (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the aid of a clear block diagram, describe the architecture of the Linear Quadratic Self-Tuning Regulator. Explain the role of the Recursive Least Squares estimator and the Riccati equation solver. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SISO plant is described by the regressor form $y(k) = -a_1 y(k-1) + b_1 u(k-1) + e(k)$ with the true parameters $a_1 = -0.8$, $b_1 = 0.5$. Write the RLS algorithm with forgetting factor $\lambda = 0.98$ to estimate $\theta = [a_1, b_1]^{T}$ and discuss why the persistent excitation condition matters for convergence. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) LQ-STR Architecture** *(7 marks)*

1. **Block-diagram description** (3 marks): The LQ-STR consists of two nested loops:

   - **Inner loop (control loop)**: $u(k) = -\hat{L}(k) x(k)$ is applied to the plant; the plant output $y(k)$ is fed back.
   - **Outer loop (identification + redesign)**: $y(k)$ and $u(k)$ enter the **regressor builder** to form $\varphi(k)$, which is processed by the **RLS estimator** to refresh $\hat{\theta}(k)$. The parameter vector is mapped to estimates $\hat{A}(k), \hat{B}(k)$, which feed the **DARE solver** to compute a fresh $\hat{P}(k)$ and then $\hat{L}(k)$. This updated gain is passed to the inner loop.

2. **Role of RLS** (2 marks): The RLS estimator produces **on-line, real-time estimates** of the unknown plant parameters from the input-output data stream. It is *recursive* (no batch storage), uses a **forgetting factor** $\lambda$ to discount older data (so the estimator can track slow drift), and converges (under persistent excitation) to the true parameters exponentially.

3. **Role of the DARE solver** (2 marks): With the latest $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$, the DARE solver computes the **Riccati matrix $\hat{P}$** that makes the LQ cost stationary. The DARE must be re-solved at every step because the gain must be consistent with the latest model — this re-solving is what gives the regulator its *self-tuning* property. The solver then produces $\hat{L}$, which is used in the inner loop.

---

**(b) RLS Algorithm and Persistent Excitation** *(7 marks)*

1. **Regressor identification** (1 mark): With $\varphi(k) = \begin{bmatrix} -y(k-1) \\ u(k-1) \end{bmatrix}$ and $\theta = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ b_1 \end{bmatrix}$, the model reads $y(k) = \varphi^{T}(k) \theta + e(k)$.

2. **RLS initialization** (1 mark): $\hat{\theta}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $P(0) = 1000 \, I_2$.

3. **Update equations** (2 marks):

   - **Gain vector**: $K(k) = \dfrac{P(k-1) \varphi(k)}{0.98 + \varphi^{T}(k) P(k-1) \varphi(k)}$
   - **Parameter update**: $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k) \left[ y(k) - \varphi^{T}(k) \hat{\theta}(k-1) \right]$
   - **Covariance update**: $P(k) = \dfrac{1}{0.98} \left[ I - K(k) \varphi^{T}(k) \right] P(k-1)$

4. **Steady-state expectation** (1 mark): As $k \to \infty$ under persistent excitation, $\hat{\theta}(k) \to \begin{bmatrix} -0.8 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$.

5. **Persistent excitation** (2 marks): A signal is **persistently exciting of order $n$** if the limit  
   $\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \varphi(k) \varphi^{T}(k)$ is **finite and positive definite**. Without this condition, the RLS covariance $P(k)$ may not decay to zero, the parameter estimate may not converge, and the closed-loop LQ-STR may exhibit *bursting* or *drift* instability. In practice, engineers superimpose a small **dither signal** (e.g., a low-amplitude PRBS — Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence) on $u(k)$ to guarantee persistent excitation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — LQ-STR**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to substitute estimates into the DARE** — Many students write the DARE with the *true* $A, B$ instead of the *estimated* $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$. The defining feature of an STR is that the design uses the **estimates**, not the true values. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Omitting the certainty equivalence statement** — KTU examiners explicitly test whether the student *knows the principle behind the design*. Always write: "By the certainty equivalence principle, the controller uses the current estimates as if they were true."
>
> 3. **Confusing the DARE with the Lyapunov equation** — They look similar but the DARE has the quadratic $A^{T} P B (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A$ correction term. Do not omit it.
>
> 4. **Forgetting stabilizability / detectability assumptions** — The DARE has a stabilising positive-definite solution only when $(\hat{A}, \hat{B})$ is stabilisable. Mention this.
>
> 5. **Neglecting the observer question** — If the question does *not* give you the state, you must add an observer $\hat{x}(k+1) = \hat{A}\hat{x}(k) + \hat{B} u(k) + K_o (y(k) - C\hat{x}(k))$ and use $\hat{x}$ in the control law. Skipping this is a common 2-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear Quadratic STR**
>
> - **LQ-STR = indirect adaptive controller** with two interacting engines: **RLS estimator** + **DARE solver**.
> - **LQ cost**: $J = \sum \left[ x^{T} Q x + u^{T} R u \right]$, with $Q \succeq 0$, $R \succ 0$.
> - **DARE (canonical form to memorise)**: $A^{T} P A - P - A^{T} P B (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A + Q = 0$.
> - **Optimal gain**: $L = (R + B^{T} P B)^{-1} B^{T} P A$.
> - **Control law**: $u(k) = -L x(k)$. Use observer $\hat{x}$ if $x$ is not measurable.
> - **RLS trio**: $K(k) = \frac{P(k-1) \varphi(k)}{\lambda + \varphi^{T}(k) P(k-1) \varphi(k)}$; $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k) \varepsilon(k)$; $P(k) = \frac{1}{\lambda}[I - K(k) \varphi^{T}(k)] P(k-1)$.
> - **Certainty equivalence**: the controller behaves as if $\hat{\theta} = \theta_{\text{true}}$.
> - **Assumptions for the LQ design to be valid**: stabilisability of $(\hat{A}, \hat{B})$ and detectability of $(\hat{A}, Q^{1/2})$.
> - **Persistent excitation** is essential for RLS convergence — use dither (e.g., PRBS) if necessary.
> - **Three-stage architecture**: Estimate → Design (DARE) → Apply (control).
> - **Implementation note**: DARE must be re-solved at every $k$ in an STR; computationally efficient solvers (e.g., Schur / eigenvector method) are used in practice.
> - **Tuning knobs**: $Q$ (penalises state error), $R$ (penalises control effort), $\lambda$ (estimator memory), $P(0)$ (initial covariance).
> - **Common engineering use**: aerospace autopilots, robotic manipulators, chemical reactors, turbine governors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Predictive Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Predictive Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Predictive Control (APC)** is a class of advanced control strategies that combine the principles of **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** with **on-line parameter adaptation**. The controller uses an explicitly identified model of the plant to predict future outputs over a finite **prediction horizon** $N_2$, optimizes a cost function over a **control horizon** $N_u$, and then applies only the first element of the optimal control sequence while updating the plant model recursively as new measurements become available.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, APC is formally treated as a synthesis of:
- **Predictive Control** (Horizon-based optimization)
- **Adaptive Estimation** (Recursive parameter identification)

The most widely studied APC algorithm is the **Generalized Predictive Control (GPC)** developed by Clarke, Mohtadi, and Tuffs (1987), which uses a **Controlled Auto-Regressive Integrated Moving-Average (CARIMA)** model and Diophantine equation-based multi-step predictors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The GPS Self-Learning Driver"**
> Imagine a driver planning a 5-km route through an unknown city. Before each turn, the GPS (predictive model) **forecasts** the road 500 m ahead based on current traffic data. The driver **optimizes** the steering and acceleration to follow the planned path while avoiding obstacles. As the trip progresses, the GPS continuously **learns** from observed traffic patterns (adaptive estimation) and re-plans the remaining route. This rolling re-planning is the essence of **receding horizon control**, and the continuous learning is what makes it **adaptive**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters

The following standardized quantities govern every APC algorithm:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $N_1$ | Minimum costing horizon | $1$ |
| $N_2$ | Maximum / prediction horizon | $5$–$25$ |
| $N_u$ | Control horizon | $1$–$5$ |
| $\lambda$ | Control weighting factor | $0$–$1$ |
| $\alpha$ | Reference trajectory softening | $0$–$1$ |
| $T_s$ | Sampling period | Application specific |

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Exam Insight:** Examiners almost always test the role of $N_1$, $N_2$, $N_u$, and $\lambda$. Memorize the effect of each on stability, speed of response, and control effort.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Philosophy — Three Pillars

1. **Explicit Prediction Model** — The controller internally maintains a model that predicts the plant output $\hat{y}(t+k \vert t)$ for $k$ steps ahead.
2. **Finite-Horizon Optimization** — At each sampling instant, an open-loop optimal control sequence is computed by minimizing a quadratic cost function $J$.
3. **Receding Horizon (Moving Horizon) Principle** — Only the first control move $u(t)$ is applied; the horizon slides forward by one step and the optimization is repeated.

### 2.2 The CARIMA Plant Model

The cornerstone of GPC is the **CARIMA** model (used because it inherently accommodates load disturbances and gives integral action):

$$
A(z^{-1}) y(t) = B(z^{-1}) z^{-d} u(t-1) + \frac{C(z^{-1})}{\Delta} \xi(t)
$$

where:
- $A(z^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_{n_a} z^{-n_a}$
- $B(z^{-1}) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \dots + b_{n_b} z^{-n_b}$
- $C(z^{-1}) = 1 + c_1 z^{-1} + \dots + c_{n_c} z^{-n_c}$ (often set to $1$)
- $\Delta = 1 - z^{-1}$ is the differencing operator
- $d$ is the **minimum plant dead time**
- $\xi(t)$ is a zero-mean white-noise disturbance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The role of $\Delta$ is critical: it guarantees **zero steady-state offset** for step references/disturbances without needing explicit integral action in the controller.

### 2.3 Diophantine Equation Decomposition

For multi-step prediction, the plant is decomposed via two successive Diophantine equations.

**Equation 1 — Filtering for $\Delta A$:**
$$
1 = E_j(z^{-1}) \tilde{A}(z^{-1}) + z^{-j} F_j(z^{-1})
$$
where $\tilde{A}(z^{-1}) = \Delta A(z^{-1})$.

**Equation 2 — Splitting $B E_j$ into past/future parts:**
$$
E_j(z^{-1}) B(z^{-1}) = G_j(z^{-1}) + z^{-j} \Gamma_j(z^{-1})
$$

### 2.4 Multi-Step Predictor

Combining the two decompositions, the optimal $j$-step ahead predictor is:

$$
\hat{y}(t+j \vert t) = G_j(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t+j-d-1) + F_j(z^{-1}) y(t) + \Gamma_j(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t-1)
$$

For implementation, this is split into:
- **Free response** $f(t+j)$ — depends on past inputs/outputs (known at time $t$)
- **Forced response** $g_j \Delta u(t+j-d-1)$ — depends on future controls

$$
\hat{y}(t+j \vert t) = f(t+j) + \sum_{i=0}^{j-d-1} g_i \, \Delta u(t+j-d-1-i)
$$

### 2.5 Quadratic Cost Function

$$
J = \sum_{j=N_1}^{N_2} \left[ \hat{y}(t+j \vert t) - w(t+j) \right]^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{N_u} \lambda \left[ \Delta u(t+j-1) \right]^2
$$

The vector form is:

$$
J = (G \tilde{u} + f - w)^T (G \tilde{u} + f - w) + \lambda \tilde{u}^T \tilde{u}
$$

### 2.6 Optimal Control Law (Closed-Form)

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \tilde{u}} = 0$ yields the unconstrained analytical solution:

$$
\tilde{u}^{\,*} = \left( G^T G + \lambda I \right)^{-1} G^T (w - f)
$$

Only the **first element** is applied, and the horizon recedes. For the scalar-input case with $N_u = 1$, this simplifies to the celebrated one-step GPC law:

$$
\Delta u(t) = \frac{g^T (w - f)}{g^T g + \lambda}
$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tilde{A}(z^{-1}) = \Delta A(z^{-1})$ | Provides integral action; used in adaptive thermal, motion, and process control |
| 2 | $1 = E_j \tilde{A} + z^{-j} F_j$ | Diophantine Eq. 1 — extracts $j$-step truncated noise/pole filter |
| 3 | $E_j B = G_j + z^{-j} \Gamma_j$ | Diophantine Eq. 2 — separates known past from controllable future |
| 4 | $\hat{y}(t+j \vert t) = G_j \Delta u(\cdot) + F_j y(t) + \Gamma_j \Delta u(t-1)$ | $j$-step optimal predictor; core of MPC |
| 5 | $J = \sum ( \hat{y} - w )^2 + \lambda \sum (\Delta u)^2$ | Penalizes tracking error and control effort — used in quadrotor, robotics |
| 6 | $\Delta u(t) = g^T(w - f) / (g^T g + \lambda)$ | Single-step control law; deployed in industrial GPC controllers |
| 7 | $\theta(t) = \theta(t-1) + K(t) [y(t) - \phi^T(t) \theta(t-1)]$ | RLS parameter update — keeps model accurate in adaptive MPC |
| 8 | $P(t) = \frac{1}{\mu} \left[ P(t-1) - \frac{P(t-1) \phi \phi^T P(t-1)}{\mu + \phi^T P(t-1) \phi} \right]$ | RLS covariance update; tunable via forgetting factor $\mu$ |
| 9 | $K(t) = \frac{P(t-1) \phi(t)}{\mu + \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)}$ | RLS gain vector |
| 10 | $w(t+j) = \alpha^j y(t) + (1 - \alpha^j) y_r$ | Reference trajectory softening (smooth setpoint) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** APC is used in **oil refineries (model predictive control of distillation columns)**, **autonomous vehicles (path prediction)**, **chemical batch reactors**, **robotic manipulators**, and **smart-grid load-frequency control**.

### 2.8 Why and How — Logical Decomposition

- **Why use $\Delta$ in CARIMA?** To achieve *offset-free* tracking without a separate integrator.
- **Why two Diophantine equations?** The first removes the $A$-polynomial dynamics from the predictor; the second isolates the controllable future terms.
- **Why $N_1 \geq d+1$?** To avoid penalizing outputs before the control action can possibly affect them.
- **Why $\lambda > 0$?** To penalize aggressive moves, ensuring smoother control and numerical conditioning of $(G^T G + \lambda I)^{-1}$.
- **Why adapt the model?** Plant parameters drift (aging, load changes, nonlinearities); RLS keeps the predictor accurate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Diophantine Equation 1

Starting from the CARIMA model multiplied by $E_j(z^{-1}) z^j$:

$$
E_j(z^{-1}) A(z^{-1}) \Delta z^j \, y(t) = E_j(z^{-1}) B(z^{-1}) z^{j-d} \Delta u(t-1) + E_j(z^{-1}) C(z^{-1}) z^j \, \xi(t)
$$

Rearranging using the identity $1 = E_j \tilde{A} + z^{-j} F_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t+j) &= G_j(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t+j-d-1) + F_j(z^{-1}) y(t) \\
&\quad + \Gamma_j(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t-1) + E_j(z^{-1}) C(z^{-1}) \xi(t+j)
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the expectation (since $E\{\xi(t+j)\}=0$ for $j>0$) gives the **optimal $j$-step predictor**:

$$
\hat{y}(t+j \vert t) = G_j(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t+j-d-1) + F_j(z^{-1}) y(t) + \Gamma_j(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t-1)
$$

**Logic:** The future white-noise terms vanish by expectation, leaving only the deterministic components (future control via $G_j$, past data via $F_j$ and $\Gamma_j$).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Control Law (Vector Form)

Collect the $N_2 - N_1 + 1$ predictions into a vector $\hat{Y}$:

$$
\hat{Y} = G \tilde{U} + f
$$

The cost in matrix form is:

$$
J = (\hat{Y} - W)^T (\hat{Y} - W) + \lambda \tilde{U}^T \tilde{U}
$$

Substitute the prediction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= (G \tilde{U} + f - W)^T (G \tilde{U} + f - W) + \lambda \tilde{U}^T \tilde{U} \\
  &= \tilde{U}^T G^T G \tilde{U} + 2 \tilde{U}^T G^T (f - W) + (f - W)^T(f - W) + \lambda \tilde{U}^T \tilde{U}
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\tilde{U}$ and set to zero (gradient condition):

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \tilde{U}} = 2 G^T G \tilde{U} + 2 G^T (f - W) + 2 \lambda \tilde{U} = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
(G^T G + \lambda I) \tilde{U} = G^T (W - f)
$$

$$
\boxed{\tilde{U}^{*} = (G^T G + \lambda I)^{-1} G^T (W - f)}
$$

Only the first row is applied: $\Delta u(t) = [1 \; 0 \; 0 \; \dots]\, \tilde{U}^{*}$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — RLS Identification Block

Define the regressor vector and parameter vector:

$$
\phi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} -y(t-1) & \dots & -y(t-n_a) \\ \Delta u(t-d-1) & \dots & \Delta u(t-d-n_b) \end{bmatrix}^T, \quad \theta = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ \vdots \\ a_{n_a} \\ b_0 \\ \vdots \\ b_{n_b} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Plant prediction:
$$
\hat{y}(t) = \phi^T(t) \theta(t-1)
$$

**RLS Update Equations (with forgetting factor $\mu$, $0.95 \leq \mu \leq 1$):**

$$
K(t) = \frac{P(t-1) \phi(t)}{\mu + \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)}
$$

$$
\theta(t) = \theta(t-1) + K(t) \left[ y(t) - \phi^T(t) \theta(t-1) \right]
$$

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\mu} \left[ P(t-1) - K(t) \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix $P(t)$ represents the *inverse* of the data covariance. It shrinks when new information is rich and grows when measurements are uninformative, naturally tracking parameter drift.

### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** For a plant with $\tilde{A}(z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.8 z^{-1}$ and $B(z^{-1}) = 0.4 z^{-1}$, compute $E_2$ and $F_2$ for the Diophantine equation $1 = E_2 \tilde{A} + z^{-2} F_2$.

**Solution:** Assume $E_2 = e_0 + e_1 z^{-1}$ and $F_2 = f_0 + f_1 z^{-1}$.

$$
E_2 \tilde{A} = (e_0 + e_1 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) = e_0 + (e_1 - 0.8 e_0) z^{-1} - 0.8 e_1 z^{-2}
$$

Add $z^{-2} F_2 = f_0 z^{-2} + f_1 z^{-3}$. Equating coefficients to $1$:

| Power of $z^{-1}$ | Coefficient | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $1$ | $e_0 = 1$ |
| $1$ | $0$ | $e_1 - 0.8 e_0 = 0 \Rightarrow e_1 = 0.8$ |
| $2$ | $0$ | $-0.8 e_1 + f_0 = 0 \Rightarrow f_0 = 0.64$ |
| $3$ | $0$ | $f_1 = 0$ |

Therefore:
$$
E_2(z^{-1}) = 1 + 0.8 z^{-1}, \qquad F_2(z^{-1}) = 0.64
$$

This satisfies $1 = (1 + 0.8 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) + z^{-2}(0.64) = 1 - 0.64 z^{-2} + 0.64 z^{-2} = 1$. ✔
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Adaptive Predictive Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r[Reference w_t] --> sum1((+))
    sum1 --> cost[Cost Function J]
    pastbuf[Past I/O Buffer y_t, u_t] --> ident[RLS Estimator]
    ident --> model[Plant Model theta_t]
    model --> pred[Multi-Step Predictor]
    pastbuf --> pred
    pred --> f[Free Response f]
    pred --> Gmat[G Matrix]
    Gmat --> cost
    f --> cost
    r --> cost
    cost --> opt[Quadratic Optimizer]
    opt --> apply[Apply First u_t]
    apply --> plant[Plant]
    plant --> ymeas[y_t+1 measured]
    ymeas --> pastbuf
    ymeas --> ident
    ymeas --> sum1
```

**Functional flow:** The RLS estimator maintains a current parameter set $\theta(t)$. The multi-step predictor uses $\theta(t)$ together with past I/O to compute the free response $f$ and the dynamic matrix $G$. The cost block $J$ uses the reference $w$, the free response $f$, and the $G$-matrix to compute the optimal future control moves. Only the first move is applied; the plant output is measured, the buffer and estimator are updated, and the cycle repeats.

### 4.2 Receding Horizon Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Receding Horizon Principle
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section At time t
    Optimize 1..5     :a1, 0, 4
    Apply u_t          :a2, 0, 1
    section At time t+1
    Optimize 2..6     :a3, 1, 5
    Apply u_t+1        :a4, 1, 2
    section At time t+2
    Optimize 3..7     :a5, 2, 6
    Apply u_t+2        :a6, 2, 3
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ID[Identification Layer]
        RLS[RLS Algorithm with Forgetting Factor]
        Cov[Parameter Covariance P_t]
    end
    subgraph PRED[Prediction Layer]
        Dio[Diophantine Solver]
        Free[Free Response Builder]
        DynG[Dynamic Matrix G Constructor]
    end
    subgraph OPT[Optimization Layer]
        Quad[Quadratic Program Solver]
        Cons[Constraint Handler]
    end
    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        Sel[First-Element Selector]
        Recede[Horizon Receder]
    end
    RLS --> Dio
    Cov --> RLS
    Dio --> Free
    Dio --> DynG
    Free --> Quad
    DynG --> Quad
    Quad --> Cons
    Cons --> Sel
    Sel --> Recede
    Recede --> RLS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Adaptive Predictive Control? How does it differ from a standard Model Predictive Controller?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adaptive Predictive Control (APC) is an advanced control strategy that **combines model predictive control with on-line parameter estimation** to handle plants with time-varying or uncertain dynamics. The key difference from standard MPC is that in APC, the internal model parameters are **updated at every sampling instant** using recursive identification (typically RLS), whereas in standard MPC the model is **assumed fixed and known** a priori. APC thereby becomes suitable for slowly time-varying, nonlinear-operating-point, or drifting processes (1 mark). Both share the receding horizon principle and the quadratic cost optimization (1 mark). APC adds an identification loop that keeps the predictor accurate (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the differencing operator $\Delta = 1 - z^{-1}$ in the CARIMA model used in GPC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The operator $\Delta$ is used in the CARIMA model $A(z^{-1}) y(t) = B(z^{-1}) z^{-d} u(t-1) + \frac{C(z^{-1})}{\Delta} \xi(t)$ to provide **built-in integral action** (1 mark). This integral action ensures **zero steady-state error** when the plant is subjected to step changes in reference or constant load disturbances, without requiring the designer to add an explicit integrator (1 mark). Additionally, $\Delta$ allows the GPC to handle plants with non-stationary disturbances more naturally and simplifies the structure of the controller transfer function (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the $j$-step ahead output predictor for a CARIMA plant model using the two Diophantine equations. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Formulate the Generalized Predictive Control cost function and obtain the closed-form optimal control law. Discuss how the control weighting factor $\lambda$ and the control horizon $N_u$ influence the closed-loop response. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the $j$-step predictor (7 marks):**

Starting from the CARIMA plant (1 mark):
$$
A(z^{-1}) y(t) = B(z^{-1}) z^{-d} u(t-1) + \frac{C(z^{-1})}{\Delta} \xi(t)
$$

Multiply by $\Delta$ to define $\tilde{A}(z^{-1}) = \Delta A(z^{-1})$ (1 mark):
$$
\tilde{A}(z^{-1}) y(t) = B(z^{-1}) z^{-d} \Delta u(t-1) + C(z^{-1}) \xi(t)
$$

**Diophantine Equation 1** (1 mark): for any prediction horizon $j$, there exist unique polynomials $E_j$ of degree $j-1$ and $F_j$ such that:
$$
1 = E_j(z^{-1}) \tilde{A}(z^{-1}) + z^{-j} F_j(z^{-1})
$$

Multiplying the CARIMA model by $E_j(z^{-1}) z^j$ and using the identity (1 mark):
$$
y(t+j) = E_j B z^{j-d} \Delta u(t+j-1) + F_j y(t) + E_j C \xi(t+j)
$$

**Diophantine Equation 2** (1 mark): split the future-input term into past ($z^{-j}$ part) and future parts:
$$
E_j(z^{-1}) B(z^{-1}) = G_j(z^{-1}) + z^{-j} \Gamma_j(z^{-1})
$$

Substituting and using $E\{\xi(t+j)\}=0$ for $j>0$ (assumption: zero-mean white noise) (1 mark):
$$
\hat{y}(t+j \vert t) = G_j(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t+j-d-1) + F_j(z^{-1}) y(t) + \Gamma_j(z^{-1}) \Delta u(t-1)
$$

This is the **optimal $j$-step ahead predictor** (1 mark). It contains three terms: future control contribution $G_j \Delta u$, past output $F_j y(t)$, and past control $\Gamma_j \Delta u(t-1)$.

---

**(b) Cost function and optimal control law (7 marks):**

Cost function definition (1 mark):
$$
J(N_1, N_2, N_u) = \sum_{j=N_1}^{N_2} \left[ \hat{y}(t+j \vert t) - w(t+j) \right]^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{N_u} \lambda \left[ \Delta u(t+j-1) \right]^2
$$

Vector representation (1 mark): let $\hat{Y} = G \tilde{U} + f$, where $f$ is the free-response vector and $G$ is the dynamic matrix.

$$
J = (G \tilde{U} + f - W)^T (G \tilde{U} + f - W) + \lambda \tilde{U}^T \tilde{U}
$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\tilde{U}$ (1 mark):
$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \tilde{U}} = 2 G^T G \tilde{U} + 2 G^T (f - W) + 2 \lambda \tilde{U} = 0
$$

Optimal control (1 mark):
$$
\tilde{U}^{*} = (G^T G + \lambda I)^{-1} G^T (W - f)
$$

Applied law: $\Delta u(t) = [1 \; 0 \; \dots]\, \tilde{U}^{*}$, then $u(t) = u(t-1) + \Delta u(t)$ (1 mark).

**Influence of $\lambda$ and $N_u$** (2 marks):
- $\lambda$: larger $\lambda$ → smoother control, slower response, more conservative; smaller $\lambda$ → aggressive, faster, may excite unmodeled dynamics.
- $N_u$: larger $N_u$ → more degrees of freedom in optimization, faster response, more computational load; $N_u = 1$ reduces to the simple one-step law $\Delta u(t) = g^T(w-f)/(g^T g + \lambda)$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an Adaptive Predictive Controller with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) identifier with forgetting factor. (7 marks)

**(b)** A plant is described by $\tilde{A}(z^{-1}) = 1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2}$ and $B(z^{-1}) = 0.5 + 0.3 z^{-1}$ with dead time $d=1$. Compute the polynomials $E_3(z^{-1})$, $F_3(z^{-1})$, $G_3(z^{-1})$, and $\Gamma_3(z^{-1})$ for GPC. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) APC architecture and RLS role (7 marks):**

**Architecture description (4 marks):** The APC consists of four coupled blocks:
1. **RLS Identifier:** Maintains an on-line estimate of the CARIMA parameters $\theta(t) = [a_1, \dots, a_{n_a}, b_0, \dots, b_{n_b}]^T$.
2. **Diophantine Solver:** Uses current $\theta(t)$ to compute the predictor polynomials $E_j, F_j, G_j, \Gamma_j$.
3. **Optimization Block:** Minimizes the cost $J$ to obtain the optimal future control sequence.
4. **Application Block:** Applies only the first move $u(t)$ and recedes the horizon by one step.

**Role of RLS with forgetting factor (3 marks):** The standard RLS update is (1 mark):
$$
K(t) = \frac{P(t-1) \phi(t)}{\mu + \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)}
$$
$$
\theta(t) = \theta(t-1) + K(t) e(t)
$$
$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\mu} \left[ P(t-1) - K(t) \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \right]
$$

The **forgetting factor $\mu$** ($0.95 \leq \mu \leq 1$) exponentially discounts old data (1 mark). When $\mu < 1$, the effective data window is approximately $1/(1-\mu)$ samples. This enables the estimator to **track slowly varying plant parameters** (e.g., due to aging, load changes, or operating-point shifts) and prevents *covariance wind-up* when excitation is poor (1 mark). Typical choices: $\mu = 0.98$ for slowly varying processes, $\mu = 0.95$ for faster drift. $\mu = 1$ gives pure least squares (no forgetting) suitable only for time-invariant plants.

---

**(b) Diophantine polynomial computation (7 marks):**

**Equation 1:** $1 = E_3 \tilde{A} + z^{-3} F_3$
Let $E_3 = e_0 + e_1 z^{-1} + e_2 z^{-2}$ and $F_3 = f_0 + f_1 z^{-1} + f_2 z^{-2}$ (1 mark for setup).

Expand $E_3 \tilde{A}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_3 \tilde{A} &= (e_0 + e_1 z^{-1} + e_2 z^{-2})(1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2}) \\
&= e_0 + (e_1 - 1.5 e_0) z^{-1} + (e_2 - 1.5 e_1 + 0.7 e_0) z^{-2} \\
&\quad + (-1.5 e_2 + 0.7 e_1) z^{-3} + 0.7 e_2 z^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Add $z^{-3} F_3$ and equate to $1$ (coefficient matching, 2 marks):

| $z^{-1}$ power | Equation | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $e_0 = 1$ | $e_0 = 1$ |
| $1$ | $e_1 - 1.5 = 0$ | $e_1 = 1.5$ |
| $2$ | $e_2 - 1.5(1.5) + 0.7 = 0$ | $e_2 = 1.55$ |
| $3$ | $-1.5(1.55) + 0.7(1.5) + f_0 = 0$ | $f_0 = 1.275$ |
| $4$ | $0.7(1.55) + f_1 = 0$ | $f_1 = -1.085$ |
| $5$ | $f_2 = 0$ | $f_2 = 0$ |

Thus: $E_3(z^{-1}) = 1 + 1.5 z^{-1} + 1.55 z^{-2}$ and $F_3(z^{-1}) = 1.275 - 1.085 z^{-1}$ (1 mark).

**Equation 2:** $E_3 B = G_3 + z^{-3} \Gamma_3$ (with $B = 0.5 + 0.3 z^{-1}$) (1 mark).

Compute $E_3 B$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_3 B &= (1 + 1.5 z^{-1} + 1.55 z^{-2})(0.5 + 0.3 z^{-1}) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.8 z^{-1} + 1.075 z^{-2} + 0.465 z^{-3} + 0.465 z^{-3} \text{? no...} 
\end{aligned}
$$

Re-compute carefully:
- $z^0$: $1 \times 0.5 = 0.5$
- $z^{-1}$: $1 \times 0.3 + 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.3 + 0.75 = 1.05$
- $z^{-2}$: $1.5 \times 0.3 + 1.55 \times 0.5 = 0.45 + 0.775 = 1.225$
- $z^{-3}$: $1.55 \times 0.3 = 0.465$

So $E_3 B = 0.5 + 1.05 z^{-1} + 1.225 z^{-2} + 0.465 z^{-3}$.

Since $d=1$ and $j=3$, the degree of $G_3$ is $j-d-1 = 1$, so $G_3 = g_0 + g_1 z^{-1}$ (1 mark).

- $g_0 = 0.5$
- $g_1 = 1.05$
- $\Gamma_3$ has the remaining terms shifted by $z^{-3}$: $\Gamma_3 = 1.225 + 0.465 z^{-1}$ (1 mark).

Therefore:
$$
\boxed{E_3 = 1 + 1.5 z^{-1} + 1.55 z^{-2}, \quad F_3 = 1.275 - 1.085 z^{-1}}
$$
$$
\boxed{G_3 = 0.5 + 1.05 z^{-1}, \quad \Gamma_3 = 1.225 + 0.465 z^{-1}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $\Delta$** before applying Diophantine Equation 1 — the prediction formula collapses if you forget to define $\tilde{A} = \Delta A$ (loss of 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up the $G_j$ and $\Gamma_j$ roles** — $G_j$ multiplies *future* control increments, $\Gamma_j$ multiplies *past* control increments. Confusing them breaks the predictor and gives a wrong optimization (loss of 2–3 marks).
> 3. **Skipping the $E\{\xi(t+j)\}=0$ justification** — this is what makes the predictor *optimal*. Examiners explicitly test this assumption (loss of 1 mark).
> 4. **Forgetting $z^{-d}$ in the control path** — the plant dead time $d$ shifts the $B$ polynomial in the prediction; ignoring it shifts all future predictions by $d$ samples (loss of 1–2 marks).
> 5. **Wrong horizon indexing in the cost** — using $\hat{y}(t+j-1)$ instead of $\hat{y}(t+j)$ shifts the cost window. Use $j \in [N_1, N_2]$ consistently.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Adaptive Predictive Control**

- **CARIMA** = $A y(t) = B z^{-d} u(t-1) + (C/\Delta)\xi(t)$. The $\Delta$ gives integral action and offset-free tracking.
- **Two Diophantine equations** are needed: $1 = E_j \tilde{A} + z^{-j} F_j$ and $E_j B = G_j + z^{-j} \Gamma_j$.
- **$j$-step predictor:** $\hat{y}(t+j \vert t) = G_j \Delta u(\cdot) + F_j y(t) + \Gamma_j \Delta u(t-1)$. Only the first two terms contain future controls.
- **Cost function** is quadratic in tracking error and control increments; weights are tunable by $\lambda$.
- **Optimal control law:** $\tilde{U}^{*} = (G^T G + \lambda I)^{-1} G^T (W - f)$. Only the first element is applied.
- **Receding horizon:** at each step, optimize over $[t, t+N_2]$, apply $\Delta u(t)$, then re-optimize from $t+1$.
- **Adaptive layer** = RLS with forgetting factor $\mu \in [0.95, 1]$ updating the $A$ and $B$ parameters every sample.
- **Parameters to tune:** $N_1 \geq d+1$, $N_2$ ≈ rise-time samples, $N_u \in \{1, \dots, 5\}$, $\lambda$ for control effort, $\mu$ for tracking speed.
- **Numerical-conditioning safety:** $\lambda > 0$ guarantees $(G^T G + \lambda I)^{-1}$ exists even if $G$ is rank-deficient.
- **Stability insight:** large $N_2$ + small $N_u$ + sufficient $\lambda$ approximates infinite-horizon LQR, improving closed-loop robustness.
- **Constraints:** GPC's quadratic law handles input/output constraints via QP; basic one-step form does not.
- **Implementation loop order:** measure $\rightarrow$ RLS update $\rightarrow$ solve Diophantine $\rightarrow$ build $G, f$ $\rightarrow$ solve QP $\rightarrow$ apply $u(t)$.
- **Practical application domains:** refining/petrochemical (DMC, IDCOM), servo motion control, autonomous vehicle trajectory tracking, drone attitude control, HVAC, and smart-grid frequency regulation.
- **Key historical reference algorithm:** Generalized Predictive Control (GPC) — Clarke, Mohtadi, Tuffs (1987) — built on CARIMA + receding horizon.
- **Common KTU traps:** not multiplying by $\Delta$, swapping $G_j$ and $\Gamma_j$, using $N_1 = 1$ for plants with $d > 0$, setting $\mu = 1$ for drifting plants, ignoring the dead time $d$ in the predictor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The MIT Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The MIT Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The MIT Rule** is a classical **direct (implicit) adaptive control law** developed at the MIT Instrumentation Laboratory in the early 1960s. It is a heuristic parameter-adjustment law that updates controller parameters **proportionally to the negative gradient of a scalar loss function** (typically the squared output error), without requiring an explicit process model identification step.

The MIT Rule is formally defined by the gradient descent update equation:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \dfrac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma \, e(t) \dfrac{\partial e(t)}{\partial \theta}}$$

where:
- $\theta$ is the adjustable controller parameter (vector)
- $J(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{2} e^2(t)$ is the squared-error loss function
- $e(t) = y_m(t) - y(t)$ is the model-following error (reference model output minus plant output)
- $\gamma > 0$ is the **adaptation gain** (a positive design constant)
- $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ is the **sensitivity derivative** of the error with respect to the parameter

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Teaching a Blindfolded Archer" Analogy**
>
> Imagine an archer wearing a blindfold who must hit a moving target (the desired response). Every time an arrow is shot, the coach calls out "miss by X inches to the left." The archer's brain computes how much to adjust his bow angle: "if I was 2 inches too far left, then increase my pull by a small amount proportional to the miss." Over time, through many small corrections, the archer's shots converge to the target.
>
> In the MIT Rule:
> - **Archer** → **Controller being adapted**
> - **Coach's call (miss distance)** → **Error signal $e(t)$**
> - **Small correction amount** → **Adaptation gain $\gamma$**
> - **"How much my miss depends on bow angle"** → **Sensitivity derivative $\partial e/\partial\theta$**
> - **Bow angle** → **Parameter $\theta$**

The archer doesn't need a full mathematical model of wind, arrow physics, or distance — only the *sign* and *magnitude* of the current error, plus a local sensitivity estimate. This is the essence of direct adaptation.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MIT Rule was the **first practical closed-loop adaptive control algorithm** to gain widespread academic and industrial recognition. It paved the way for modern Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) and forms the conceptual foundation on which **Lyapunov-based** and **passivity-based** adaptive laws are built.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The MIT Rule operates in **three explicit phases** every adaptation cycle:

1. **Error Generation Phase**
   - The reference model produces $y_m(t)$ representing the ideal desired response to $r(t)$.
   - The actual plant produces $y(t)$.
   - The **model-following error** is computed: $e(t) = y_m(t) - y(t)$.

2. **Sensitivity Computation Phase**
   - The local slope $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ is computed (or approximated) — this tells how the error *changes* with an infinitesimal parameter perturbation.
   - In practice, this is obtained by running a **sensitivity model** in parallel with the plant, or approximated by algebraic manipulation at slow adaptation rates.

3. **Parameter Update Phase**
   - The controller parameter is updated along the negative gradient:
   - $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, e(t) \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$
   - If error is positive (plant under-responding), and the error *increases* with $\theta$, then $\theta$ must be **decreased**, which the sign convention correctly dictates.

## 2.2 Why the Gradient Descent Form?

The MIT Rule is derived from a simple optimization principle: **minimize $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}e^2$** with respect to $\theta$.

For a minimum of $J$, we require $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = 0$. Steepest descent gives:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma \, e \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

The parameter $\theta$ continually moves in the direction that **most rapidly reduces** the squared error — this is why the rule converges (under slow adaptation and small error assumptions).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Equation** | **Meaning** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| $e(t) = y_m(t) - y(t)$ | Model-following error | Output units (m, rad, V, etc.) |
| $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} e^2$ | Loss / cost function | (Output)² |
| $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, e \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ | **MIT Rule (core equation)** | $\gamma$ in s⁻¹·(output)⁻¹ |
| $\gamma > 0$ | Adaptation gain (design knob) | Typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{2}$ |
| $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = -\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$ | Sensitivity derivative (since $y_m$ is $\theta$-independent) | Dimensionless if $\theta$ is dimensionless |
| $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_r$ | Feedforward / feedback / reference gains | Dimensionless |
| $T_a = 1/\gamma$ | Time-constant of adaptation | Seconds |
| $\omega_a$ | Bandwidth of adaptation loop | rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Note for KTU Examinations:**
> Since $e = y_m - y$, we have $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = -\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$. Many textbooks write the MIT Rule as $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \gamma \, e \, \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$ (with the negative absorbed), which is **algebraically identical**. Students often lose marks by writing conflicting signs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MIT Rule has been historically applied to:
- **Aircraft flight control systems** (early autopilot adaptation)
- **Electric motor speed/position regulation** (gain scheduling replacement)
- **Chemical process control** (pH, temperature loops)
- **Robotic manipulator tracking** (joint servo adaptation)
- **Power electronic converters** (voltage regulator adaptation)

While replaced in safety-critical systems by **Lyapunov-stable MRAC** and **backstepping-based adaptive control**, the MIT Rule remains in industrial pedagogical use because of its **simplicity and conceptual clarity**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Derivation of the MIT Rule

**Starting Assumption:** We wish to minimize a scalar quadratic loss function in the tracking error.

**Step 1 — Define the loss function.**
Let the tracking error be:

$$e(t) = y_m(t) - y(t)$$

Define a positive-definite scalar cost:

$$J(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{2} e^2(t)$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is purely for mathematical convenience (cancels the 2 in differentiation).

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient of the loss.**
Differentiating $J$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

This step uses the chain rule: $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}e^2\right) = e \cdot \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the steepest descent principle.**
The direction of *steepest decrease* of $J$ is $-\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$. The MIT Rule moves the parameter along this direction with proportional magnitude:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}}$$

Substituting $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = -\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = +\gamma \, e \, \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$$

Both forms are equivalent and accepted in KTU examinations.

**Step 4 — Note on slow-adaptation assumption.**
The derivation assumes that $\theta$ changes *slowly* compared to the plant dynamics, so that the sensitivity derivative $\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$ can be evaluated assuming $\theta$ is **frozen** during the transient analysis. This is the **"frozen parameter" or quasi-static assumption**, which is the key engineering limitation of the MIT Rule.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — MIT Rule for a First-Order Plant

**System Setup:**
Consider a first-order plant with unknown gain and time constant:

$$\dfrac{y(s)}{u(s)} = \dfrac{K_p}{s + a_p}$$

The reference model (desired closed-loop response) is:

$$\dfrac{y_m(s)}{r(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s + a_m}$$

A controller with two adjustable parameters is chosen:

$$u(t) = \theta_1(t) \, r(t) - \theta_2(t) \, y(t)$$

The parameter $\theta_1$ is the **feedforward gain**, and $\theta_2$ is the **feedback gain**. The ideal values (when the plant matches the model) are $\theta_1^* = K_m/K_p$ and $\theta_2^* = a_m - a_p$.

**Step A — Closed-loop plant equation.**
Substituting the controller into the plant:

$$y = \dfrac{K_p}{s + a_p} \big[ \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y \big]$$

Rearranging:

$$\big[ s + a_p + \theta_2 K_p \big] \, y = K_p \, \theta_1 \, r$$

So:

$$y = \dfrac{K_p \, \theta_1}{s + a_p + \theta_2 K_p} \, r$$

**Step B — Compute sensitivity derivatives.**
The sensitivity of $y$ with respect to $\theta_1$:

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} = \dfrac{K_p}{s + a_p + \theta_2 K_p} \, r$$

The sensitivity of $y$ with respect to $\theta_2$:

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} = -\dfrac{K_p \, \theta_1}{\big( s + a_p + \theta_2 K_p \big)^2} \, r = -\dfrac{1}{K_p \theta_1} \left( \dfrac{K_p \theta_1}{s + a_p + \theta_2 K_p} \right)^2 r$$

Under the slow-adaptation assumption, replace $\theta_1, \theta_2$ in the denominators by their nominal (initial) values. This gives linear time-invariant (LTI) **sensitivity filters**:

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} \approx \dfrac{K_p \, r(s)}{s + a_p + \theta_2^{0} K_p}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} \approx -\dfrac{K_p \, \theta_1^{0} \, y(s)}{s + a_p + \theta_2^{0} K_p}$$

**Step C — Apply MIT Rule to each parameter.**

$$\dfrac{d\theta_1}{dt} = -\gamma_1 \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_1} = \gamma_1 \, e \, \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1}$$

$$\dfrac{d\theta_2}{dt} = -\gamma_2 \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_2} = \gamma_2 \, e \, \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2}$$

Explicitly:

$$\dfrac{d\theta_1}{dt} = \gamma_1 \, e(t) \cdot \dfrac{K_p \, r(t)}{s + a_p + \theta_2^{0} K_p}$$

$$\dfrac{d\theta_2}{dt} = -\gamma_2 \, e(t) \cdot \dfrac{K_p \, \theta_1^{0} \, y(t)}{s + a_p + \theta_2^{0} K_p}$$

These constitute the **MIT Rule adaptation laws** for the first-order system.

## 3.3 Stability Limitation — The Normalized MIT Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Stability Caveat (KTU High-Yield):**
> The MIT Rule has a **known stability problem**: large $\gamma$ leads to instability, and large reference signal $r$ also destabilizes the loop. The "vanilla" rule is therefore rarely used in safety-critical applications without modification.

The most common KTU-exam-tested modification is the **Normalized MIT Rule**:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, \dfrac{e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}}{\alpha + \left( \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \right)^T \left( \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \right)}}$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is a small normalization constant preventing division by zero. This keeps the adaptation rate bounded regardless of the magnitude of $r(t)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the MIT Rule Adaptive System (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["Reference Input r(t)"]
    nodeRM["Reference Model Plant K_m over s plus a_m"]
    nodeYm["Desired Output y_m(t)"]
    nodeCMP["Summing Junction: e = y_m minus y"]
    nodeE["Error Signal e(t)"]
    nodeGAM["Adaptation Gain gamma greater than zero"]
    nodeSENS["Sensitivity Filter partial y over partial theta"]
    nodeADJ["MIT Update Law: dtheta over dt equals minus gamma e times partial e over partial theta"]
    nodeCTRL["Adjustable Controller: u equals theta_1 r minus theta_2 y"]
    nodePLNT["Unknown Plant: K_p over s plus a_p"]
    nodeY["Plant Output y(t)"]
    nodeFB["Feedback Path to Controller"]

    nodeR --> nodeRM
    nodeRM --> nodeYm
    nodeYm --> nodeCMP
    nodeY --> nodeCMP
    nodeCMP --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeGAM
    nodeGAM --> nodeADJ
    nodeSENS --> nodeADJ
    nodeADJ --> nodeCTRL
    nodeR --> nodeCTRL
    nodeFB --> nodeCTRL
    nodeCTRL --> nodePLNT
    nodePLNT --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeFB
    nodeY --> nodeSENS
    nodeR --> nodeSENS
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **outer loop** is the model-reference loop (compares $y_m$ with $y$).
- The **inner loop** is the adaptation loop: $e$ drives the parameter update through the sensitivity filter.
- The sensitivity filter $\partial y/\partial\theta$ operates in **parallel** with the plant to provide the local gradient information.

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Interpretation

| **Signal Path** | **Function** | **Direction of Information** |
|---|---|---|
| $r \rightarrow$ Controller $\rightarrow$ Plant $\rightarrow y$ | Forward path | Causal |
| $y \rightarrow$ Error comparator $\rightarrow e$ | Error generation | Causal |
| $e \rightarrow$ Adaptation law $\rightarrow \Delta\theta$ | Parameter update | Causal |
| $\theta \rightarrow$ Controller gain modification | Gain scheduling in real time | Causal |
| $r$ and $y \rightarrow$ Sensitivity filter $\rightarrow \partial y/\partial\theta$ | Sensitivity measurement | Parallel auxiliary |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

### Question 1
**State and derive the MIT Rule for adaptive control. Mention its key limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The MIT Rule is a direct adaptive control law that adjusts a controller parameter $\theta$ in the direction of the negative gradient of a squared-error loss function $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}e^2$:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

The error is $e = y_m - y$, $\gamma > 0$ is the adaptation gain. Derivation follows from steepest descent minimization of $J$. **Key limitation**: the rule can become unstable for large adaptation gain $\gamma$ or large reference signal $r(t)$; this motivates the normalized MIT Rule. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**What is a sensitivity derivative in the context of the MIT Rule? Why is the slow-adaptation assumption necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **sensitivity derivative** $\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$ measures how the plant output $y$ changes with an infinitesimal change in the controller parameter $\theta$. It is the local gradient of the plant output with respect to the parameter being adapted. **(1 mark)**

The **slow-adaptation assumption** is required because the MIT Rule derivation treats $\theta$ as a *constant* during the sensitivity computation. If $\theta$ varies rapidly, the sensitivity derivative becomes time-varying and coupled to the parameter dynamics, breaking the gradient-descent interpretation and risking instability. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU End-Semester Examination — Model Question Paper Style]**

### ⭐ Question A — Alternative 1 (14 Marks)

**Derive the MIT Rule adaptation laws for a first-order plant whose transfer function is $\dfrac{K_p}{s + a_p}$ driven by a controller $u = \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y$, with reference model $\dfrac{K_m}{s + a_m}$. Discuss stability and the role of adaptation gain.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Sensitivity Derivatives (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Write the plant equation: $s y = -a_p y + K_p u$. Substituting $u = \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y$:

$$\big( s + a_p + K_p \theta_2 \big) y = K_p \theta_1 \, r$$

Hence:

$$y = \dfrac{K_p \theta_1}{s + a_p + K_p \theta_2} \, r$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate partially with respect to $\theta_1$:

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} = \dfrac{K_p}{s + a_p + K_p \theta_2} \, r$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate partially with respect to $\theta_2$:

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} = -\dfrac{K_p^2 \theta_1}{\big( s + a_p + K_p \theta_2 \big)^2} \, r = -\dfrac{1}{K_p \theta_1} \, y^2 / r \cdot r$$

Approximated under slow adaptation (replace $\theta_1, \theta_2$ by their nominal values $\theta_1^{0}, \theta_2^{0}$):

$$\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} \approx \dfrac{K_p \, r}{s + a_p + K_p \theta_2^{0}} \quad ; \quad \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} \approx -\dfrac{K_p \, \theta_1^{0} \, y}{s + a_p + K_p \theta_2^{0}}$$

**(7 marks awarded for full derivation with sensitivity filter expressions.)**

#### Part (b) — MIT Adaptation Laws and Stability Discussion (7 marks)

The MIT Rule is applied to each parameter:

$$\dfrac{d\theta_1}{dt} = -\gamma_1 \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_1} = +\gamma_1 \, e \, \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} = \gamma_1 \, e \cdot \dfrac{K_p \, r}{s + a_p + K_p \theta_2^{0}}$$

$$\dfrac{d\theta_2}{dt} = -\gamma_2 \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_2} = +\gamma_2 \, e \, \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} = -\gamma_2 \, e \cdot \dfrac{K_p \, \theta_1^{0} \, y}{s + a_p + K_p \theta_2^{0}}$$

**Stability Discussion:**
- Stability of the MIT Rule is **not guaranteed by the derivation itself**; the rule is heuristic.
- The closed-loop poles of the *adaptation loop* depend on $\gamma$, $K_p$, $a_p$, $\theta_2^{0}$, and the spectrum of $r(t)$.
- For **small $\gamma$** and **bounded $r(t)$**, the system is stable and $e \to 0$.
- For **large $\gamma$** or **large $r(t)$**, the adaptation loop may exhibit sustained oscillations or instability.
- **Normalization** of the update law eliminates the dependence on $\lvert r \rvert$:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, \dfrac{e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}}{\alpha + \left( \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \right)^2}, \quad \alpha > 0$$

**(7 marks awarded.)**

---

### ⭐ Question B — Alternative 2 (14 Marks)

**Explain with a neat block diagram how the MIT Rule is implemented in a model-reference adaptive control system. Derive the normalized MIT Rule and justify why it is preferred over the conventional form.**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Operation (7 marks)

The MIT Rule MRAC system consists of:
1. A **reference model** generating $y_m(t)$ from $r(t)$.
2. An **adjustable controller** producing control signal $u(t) = f(r, y, \theta)$.
3. An **unknown plant** producing $y(t)$.
4. An **error summer** computing $e(t) = y_m(t) - y(t)$.
5. A **sensitivity filter** computing $\partial y / \partial \theta$ from $r$ and $y$.
6. An **adaptation mechanism** implementing $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma e \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$.

The information flow: $r$ enters both the reference model (producing $y_m$) and the controller (producing $u$). $y$ is compared with $y_m$ to form $e$. $e$ and the sensitivity $\partial e/\partial \theta$ feed the MIT update law, which adjusts $\theta$ in real time. The plant and sensitivity filter operate in parallel. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Derivation of the Normalized MIT Rule (7 marks)

The conventional MIT Rule is:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

**Problem:** If $\lvert \partial e/\partial \theta \rvert$ is large (e.g., due to a large $r$), the rate of adaptation becomes unbounded, leading to instability.

**Solution — Normalization:** Divide the update by $\left( \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \right)^2$ (and add a small $\alpha > 0$ to prevent division by zero):

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, \dfrac{e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}}{\alpha + \left( \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \right)^2}$$

**Justification:** Now the effective adaptation gain is bounded:

$$\gamma_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\alpha + (\partial e/\partial \theta)^2} \le \dfrac{\gamma}{\alpha}$$

ensuring the adaptation rate remains bounded regardless of the magnitude of $r(t)$ or the sensitivity derivative. This guarantees robust stability for a much wider range of operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on The MIT Rule:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error (–1 mark):** Forgetting that $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = -\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$ and writing inconsistent signs in the adaptation law. Always state $e = y_m - y$ explicitly at the top.
>
> 2. **Missing Slow-Adaptation Assumption (–2 marks):** The MIT Rule is derived under the assumption that parameter dynamics are slower than plant dynamics. Without stating this, the derivation is **incomplete** and examiners deduct marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing Loss Function (–1 mark):** Writing $J = e$ instead of $J = \frac{1}{2} e^2$. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is essential and mathematically standard.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Sensitivity Filter (–2 marks):** In Part B derivations, students often write only the update equation without computing the explicit sensitivity filter expression. Always derive $\partial y / \partial \theta$ explicitly.
>
> 5. **No Mention of Normalization (–2 marks):** Stability is a high-weight topic. Examiners expect at least one sentence on the **normalized MIT Rule** and its stabilizing effect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **The MIT Rule — Final Revision Snapshot**

- The MIT Rule is a **direct (implicit) adaptive law** — no explicit parameter estimation step.
- Core equation: $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = +\gamma \, e \, \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$
- Loss function: $J(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{2} e^2(t)$ with $e(t) = y_m(t) - y(t)$
- **Three components** of every MIT system: error generator, sensitivity filter, adaptation mechanism.
- **Slow-adaptation assumption** is essential; otherwise the gradient interpretation breaks down.
- **Adaptation gain $\gamma > 0$** is the only design knob — increasing it speeds convergence but degrades stability.
- **Sensitivity derivative** $\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$ is computed by linearizing the plant around the current operating point and forming a parallel sensitivity model.
- **Instability sources**: large $\gamma$, large $\lvert r(t) \rvert$, unmodelled fast dynamics, plant-model order mismatch.
- **Normalized MIT Rule**: divide by $\alpha + (\partial e / \partial \theta)^2$ — gives bounded effective adaptation gain.
- **Convergence theorem** (KTU standard): For the first-order plant, MIT Rule is **locally stable** for $0 < \gamma < \gamma_{\text{max}}$ (positive-real-condition-based bound).
- **Historical origin**: MIT Instrumentation Laboratory, early 1960s; first MRAC application was **aircraft pitch control**.
- **Modern replacement**: Lyapunov-based MRAC, $e_1$-modification, $\sigma$-modification, and composite adaptive laws — all designed to overcome the MIT Rule's stability weakness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Adaptation Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT632 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Adaptation Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptation Gain (γ):** The scalar (or matrix) positive-definite weighting parameter that scales the rate at which the controller parameters $\hat{\theta}(t)$ are updated in an adaptive law. It directly controls the trade-off between **adaptation speed** and **closed-loop robustness**.

The adaptation gain appears in the generic **gradient-type adaptation law**:

$$\hat{\theta}(t) = -\gamma \cdot e(t) \cdot \frac{\partial e(t)}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$$

where $\gamma > 0$ is the adaptation gain, $e(t)$ is the tracking/output error, and $\frac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$ is the sensitivity derivative of the error with respect to the adjustable parameter.

**Mathematical role:** In the Lyapunov-based framework, $\gamma$ enters the parameter error dynamics as a direct multiplier of the regressor–error product, governing the speed at which parameter estimates converge toward their ideal values.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Learner in a New Job"**
>
> Imagine a fresher learning a new task. The **adaptation gain** is like the **learning rate**:
>
> - **High γ (eager learner):** Learns very fast from every small mistake. But gets confused by noise, makes impulsive changes, and may "panic" — leading to instability.
> - **Low γ (cautious learner):** Learns slowly, observes more before changing. Robust to disturbances but slow to adapt to real changes.
> - **Optimal γ:** Learns fast enough to track changes, but smooth enough to ignore noise.
>
> Just as no universal "best learning rate" exists for every human, the adaptation gain must be **tuned** based on plant dynamics, noise level, and reference signal characteristics.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Adaptation Gain | $\gamma$ | Positive scalar/matrix scaling parameter update |
| Parameter Error | $\phi$ | $\phi = \hat{\theta} - \theta^*$ (deviation from ideal) |
| Tracking Error | $e$ | $e = y_m - y$ (model minus plant output) |
| Sensitivity Derivative | $\frac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$ | Gradient of error w.r.t. parameters |
| Regressor | $\omega$ | Measurable signal driving parameter adaptation |
| Normalized Gain | $\gamma_n$ | $\gamma_n = \gamma / (1 + \vert\vert \omega \vert\vert^2)$ for robustness |

### Units & Magnitude

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Units:** Depends on context — dimensionless for normalized MRAC, $[s^{-1}]$ in continuous-time equivalent form.
> - **Typical Range:** $\gamma \in [0.01,\; 100]$ — determined empirically or analytically per method.
> - **Constraint:** $\gamma > 0$ for stability (Lyapunov sign condition).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Adaptation Gain Determination Non-Trivial?

The determination of $\gamma$ is critical because:

1. **Existence-Uniqueness**: $\gamma$ must be finite and positive for the ODE describing $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to have a unique, bounded solution.
2. **Stability**: If $\gamma$ is too large, the closed-loop adaptive system becomes **unstable** even when the plant is stable and well-known.
3. **Performance**: $\gamma$ sets the **bandwidth** of the adaptation loop — a small $\gamma$ gives a sluggish adaptive loop, a large $\gamma$ gives a fast but noisy one.
4. **Robustness**: In the presence of unmodeled dynamics or disturbances, $\gamma$ must be small enough to prevent **parameter drift** and bursting.

### 2.2 Methods of Determining Adaptation Gain

Five principal approaches exist:

**(a) Empirical / Trial-and-Error Method**
Start with a small $\gamma$, incrementally increase until acceptable transient response without instability.

**(b) Routh–Hurwitz Method**
Linearize the closed-loop error dynamics and apply Routh's criterion to find the range of $\gamma$ for which all poles lie in the left-half plane.

**(c) Lyapunov Stability Method**
Construct a positive-definite Lyapunov function $V(e, \phi)$ and require $\dot{V} \le 0$. The condition naturally yields an admissible range of $\gamma$.

**(d) Hyperstability / Popov Criterion**
Treat the adaptive loop as a feedback interconnection of a linear feed-forward block and a nonlinear feedback block; apply the Popov inequality to bound $\gamma$.

**(e) Normalized / Robust Adaptation**
Replace $\gamma$ with $\gamma_n = \gamma / (1 + \vert\vert \omega \vert\vert^2)$ to make the effective gain signal-dependent and bounded.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formulas for Determination of Adaptation Gain**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\hat{\theta} = -\gamma \cdot e \cdot \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$ | MIT-rule adaptation law |
| 2 | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = \gamma \cdot e \cdot \omega$ | Gradient-based parameter update |
| 3 | $V = e^2 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma}\,\phi^T\,\phi$ | Lyapunov candidate (scalar form) |
| 4 | $\dot{V} = -2a_m e^2 \le 0$ | Negative semi-definite condition (stable) |
| 5 | $\gamma_{\max} = \dfrac{a_m}{b_{\max}\,\vert\vert \omega \vert\vert_{\max}}$ | Upper bound from Lyapunov |
| 6 | $\gamma_n = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + \vert\vert \omega \vert\vert^2}$ | Normalized gain (robust form) |
| 7 | $\dot{e} = -a_m e + b\,\phi^T \omega$ | Tracking-error dynamics (MRAC) |
| 8 | $\phi^T = -\gamma \int_0^t e(\tau)\,\omega(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Integrated form of adaptation law |
| 9 | $\dot{V} + \int_0^t e^2 d\tau \le \gamma^2 \int_0^t \omega^T \omega\, d\tau$ | Popov hyperstability inequality |
| 10 | $\text{Re}[W(j\omega)] \ge 0$ | Positive-realness of feed-forward block |

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $\gamma > 0$ for all methods (positivity)
- $a_m > 0$ (model must be stable / Hurwitz)
- Regressor $\omega$ must be **persistently exciting (PE)** for parameter convergence
- Normalization factor $1 + \vert\vert\omega\vert\vert^2 > 0$ always satisfied

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

In real engineering systems, the adaptation gain determines performance in:
- **Aerospace flight control** (parameter changes due to altitude/Mach)
- **Robotic manipulators** (load variations during payload changes)
- **Process control** (chemical reactor dynamics with catalyst aging)
- **Automotive engine control** (fuel mapping with temperature/pressure)
- **Renewable energy systems** (wind turbine pitch control)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world tip:** Industrial adaptive controllers (e.g., self-tuning regulators in pulp & paper, aerospace) usually start with $\gamma$ chosen by **Lyapunov bounds**, then **fine-tuned empirically** during commissioning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Adaptation Gain from Lyapunov Stability (First-Order MRAC)

This is the **canonical KTU derivation** — first-order plant, MIT-rule / Lyapunov-based adaptation law.

#### Step 1: Define the Plant and Reference Model

**Plant** (unknown parameters $a$, $b$):

$$\dot{y}_p = -a\,y_p + b\,u$$

**Reference Model** (known, stable):

$$\dot{y}_m = -a_m\,y_m + b_m\,r$$

with $a_m > 0$.

#### Step 2: Define the Control Law

The controller uses two adjustable parameters:

$$u(t) = \theta_1(t)\cdot r(t) - \theta_2(t)\cdot y_p(t)$$

Ideal values for perfect matching:

$$\theta_1^* = \frac{b_m}{b}, \qquad \theta_2^* = \frac{a_m - a}{b}$$

#### Step 3: Express Tracking-Error Dynamics

Define tracking error $e = y_m - y_p$. Computing $\dot{e}$:

$$\dot{e} = \dot{y}_m - \dot{y}_p = -a_m y_m + b_m r - (-a y_p + b u)$$

Substitute $u = \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y_p$:

$$\dot{e} = -a_m y_m + b_m r + a y_p - b(\theta_1 r - \theta_2 y_p)$$

Group terms:

$$\dot{e} = -a_m(y_m - y_p) + (a_m - a)y_p + b_m r - b\theta_1 r + b\theta_2 y_p$$

Wait — careful expansion. We want everything in terms of $e$, $r$, and $y_p$. Note that $y_m = e + y_p$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{e} &= -a_m(e + y_p) + b_m r + a y_p - b\theta_1 r + b\theta_2 y_p \\
&= -a_m e - a_m y_p + b_m r + a y_p - b\theta_1 r + b\theta_2 y_p \\
&= -a_m e + (a - a_m + b\theta_2)y_p + (b_m - b\theta_1)r
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4: Introduce Parameter Errors

Define:

$$\phi_1 = \theta_1 - \theta_1^* = \theta_1 - \frac{b_m}{b}, \qquad \phi_2 = \theta_2 - \theta_2^* = \theta_2 - \frac{a_m - a}{b}$$

Compute the coefficient expressions:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_m - b\theta_1 &= b_m - b(\theta_1^* + \phi_1) = b_m - b_m - b\phi_1 = -b\,\phi_1 \\
a - a_m + b\theta_2 &= a - a_m + b(\theta_2^* + \phi_2) = a - a_m + (a_m - a) + b\phi_2 = b\,\phi_2
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the error dynamics simplify to:

$$\dot{e} = -a_m\,e + b\,\phi_2\,y_p - b\,\phi_1\,r$$

Or compactly, using regressor vector $\omega = [r,\; y_p]^T$:

$$\dot{e} = -a_m\,e + b\,\phi^T\,\omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the **foundation** for all adaptation-gain analysis. It shows that the error dynamics are driven by the parameter error $\phi$ multiplied by the known regressor $\omega$.

#### Step 5: Propose Adaptation Laws (Ansatz)

We want $\phi \to 0$ and $e \to 0$. Try the form:

$$\dot{\theta}_1 = \gamma_1\,e\,r, \qquad \dot{\theta}_2 = -\gamma_2\,e\,y_p$$

Since $\theta_1^*, \theta_2^*$ are constant, $\dot{\phi}_1 = \dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\phi}_2 = \dot{\theta}_2$:

$$\dot{\phi}_1 = \gamma_1\,e\,r, \qquad \dot{\phi}_2 = -\gamma_2\,e\,y_p$$

#### Step 6: Construct the Lyapunov Function

Choose a quadratic candidate:

$$V(e, \phi_1, \phi_2) = e^2 + \frac{b}{\gamma_1}\,\phi_1^2 + \frac{b}{\gamma_2}\,\phi_2^2$$

This is **positive-definite** provided $b > 0$ and $\gamma_1, \gamma_2 > 0$.

#### Step 7: Compute $\dot{V}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= 2e\dot{e} + \frac{2b}{\gamma_1}\phi_1\dot{\phi}_1 + \frac{2b}{\gamma_2}\phi_2\dot{\phi}_2 \\
&= 2e(-a_m e + b\phi_2 y_p - b\phi_1 r) + \frac{2b}{\gamma_1}\phi_1(\gamma_1 e r) + \frac{2b}{\gamma_2}\phi_2(-\gamma_2 e y_p) \\
&= -2a_m e^2 + 2b\,e\phi_2 y_p - 2b\,e\phi_1 r + 2b\,e\phi_1 r - 2b\,e\phi_2 y_p \\
&= -2a_m e^2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\dot{V} = -2a_m e^2 \le 0$ (since $a_m > 0$).
>
> Therefore, **any positive value** of $\gamma_1, \gamma_2$ guarantees Lyapunov stability. The adaptation gain is **bounded only by the inequality $\gamma > 0$** in the ideal (no noise / no unmodeled dynamics) case.

#### Step 8: Determine Practical Upper Bound

In practice, disturbances, noise, and unmodeled dynamics impose a **maximum allowable** $\gamma$. From the Popov / hyperstability criterion:

$$\gamma_{\max} = \frac{a_m}{b_{\max}\,\vert\vert \omega \vert\vert_{\max}^2}$$

where $b_{\max}$ and $\vert\vert\omega\vert\vert_{\max}$ are the worst-case bounds on the plant gain and regressor norm.

> [!WARNING]
> Choosing $\gamma > \gamma_{\max}$ causes **parameter drift**, oscillations, and possible instability — even though the Lyapunov analysis predicts stability in the ideal case.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Methods

| Method | Computational Effort | Guarantees | Typical Use | Limitation |
|--------|---------------------|------------|-------------|------------|
| Empirical / Trial–Error | Low | None (post-hoc tuning) | Industrial commissioning | Slow, requires test rig |
| Routh–Hurwitz | Medium | Local stability (linearized) | Linear-parameter design | Conservative, validity region limited |
| Lyapunov | Medium–High | Global stability | Theoretical design | Ideal-plant assumption |
| Hyperstability / Popov | High | Robustness bounds | Aerospace, safety-critical | Complex to apply |
| Normalized Adaptation | Medium | Robust to PE violations | Real-world implementation | Slower convergence |

### 3.3 Worked Example (Numerical)

Consider a plant with $a = 2$, $b = 1$, and reference model with $a_m = 5$, $b_m = 2$. Find the ideal parameters and a valid $\gamma$.

**Step A — Ideal parameters:**

$$\theta_1^* = \frac{b_m}{b} = \frac{2}{1} = 2, \qquad \theta_2^* = \frac{a_m - a}{b} = \frac{5 - 2}{1} = 3$$

**Step B — Adaptation law:**

$$\dot{\theta}_1 = \gamma_1 e\, r, \qquad \dot{\theta}_2 = -\gamma_2 e\, y_p$$

**Step C — Choose $\gamma$:** By Lyapunov, any $\gamma_1, \gamma_2 > 0$ works. A typical choice for medium-speed adaptation: $\gamma_1 = 10$, $\gamma_2 = 10$.

**Step D — Normalized alternative:**

$$\gamma_{1,n} = \frac{10}{1 + r^2 + y_p^2}, \qquad \gamma_{2,n} = \frac{10}{1 + r^2 + y_p^2}$$

The normalized form is preferred when the regressor magnitude varies significantly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRAC Loop Showing Adaptation Gain Location

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r[Reference Signal r] --> SumNode
    SumNode[+] -->|Error e| Plant[Plant Gp]
    Plant -->|Output yp| SumNode2[Sum]
    r --> Model[Reference Model Gm]
    Model -->|Output ym| SumNode2
    SumNode2 -->|Error e| Gain[Adaptation Gain gamma]
    Gain -->|Scaled Error| AdaptLaw[Adaptation Law]
    AdaptLaw -->|Updates theta_hat| Controller[Adjustable Controller]
    Controller -->|Control u| Plant
    Plant -.->|Output yp| Sensitivity[Sensitivity Derivative]
    Sensitivity --> AdaptLaw
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow: How to Choose Adaptation Gain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need to determine gamma] --> B{Application Type?}
    B -->|Safety-critical / aerospace| C[Lyapunov Method]
    B -->|Industrial process| D[Empirical Trial-Error]
    B -->|Theoretical analysis| E[Hyperstability / Popov]
    B -->|Linear parameter regime| F[Routh-Hurwitz]
    C --> G{Noise / Disturbance?}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Use Normalized gamma_n]
    G -->|No| I[Use Fixed gamma]
    H --> J[Simulate and Validate]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Stable and Fast Enough?}
    K -->|No| L[Adjust gamma and Repeat]
    K -->|Yes| M[Finalize gamma]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Adaptive System with Adaptation Gain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Reference [Reference Path]
        r_in[R r] --> RefModel[Reference Model Gm s]
        RefModel --> ym[Output ym]
    end

    subgraph PlantPath [Plant Path]
        u_in[Control u] --> Plant[Plant Gp s unknown]
        Plant --> yp[Output yp]
    end

    subgraph ErrorCalc [Error Generation]
        ym --> DiffNode
        yp --> DiffNode
        DiffNode[Subtractor] --> err[Tracking Error e]
    end

    subgraph AdaptMech [Adaptation Mechanism]
        err --> Mult[Multiplier]
        omega[Regressor omega] --> Mult
        Mult --> Prod[e times omega]
        Prod --> Gain[Adaptation Gain gamma]
        Gain --> Integ[Integrator]
        Integ --> theta_hat[theta_hat updated]
        theta_hat --> Controller[Controller]
        Controller --> u_in
        yp --> omega
    end
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TradeOff [Adaptation Gain Trade-off]
        direction LR
        Low[Low gamma] -->|Fast Adaptation| Mid[Medium gamma Optimal]
        Mid -->|Slow Adaptation| High[High gamma]
        Low -.->|Slow but robust| Mid
        Mid -.->|Fast but noisy| High
        High -.->|Instability risk| Instable[Unstable / Divergent]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** Define the **adaptation gain** in the context of MRAC. State the conditions on its value for stability of the first-order adaptive system.

**Model Answer:**
The adaptation gain $\gamma$ is the positive scalar that scales the rate of parameter update in the adaptive law $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = \gamma \cdot e \cdot \omega$. For first-order MRAC, by Lyapunov analysis, **any** $\gamma > 0$ guarantees stability in the ideal (noise-free) case. In practice, the upper bound $\gamma_{\max} = \dfrac{a_m}{b_{\max}\,\vert\vert\omega\vert\vert_{\max}^2}$ must be respected for robustness. **[3 marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** What is **normalized adaptation gain**? Why is it used in practical MRAC systems?

**Model Answer:**
Normalized adaptation gain is defined as $\gamma_n = \dfrac{\gamma}{1 + \vert\vert\omega\vert\vert^2}$. It is used because the standard (unnormalized) adaptation gain can lead to **parameter drift and instability** when the regressor $\omega$ is large. Normalization ensures the **effective gain** remains bounded regardless of signal magnitudes, providing robustness to disturbances, unmodeled dynamics, and loss of persistent excitation. **[3 marks]**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Consider the first-order plant $\dot{y}_p = -2 y_p + 3u$ and reference model $\dot{y}_m = -5 y_m + 6r$. Design a Lyapunov-based MRAC and determine the range of adaptation gains $\gamma_1, \gamma_2$ that guarantee asymptotic stability of the error dynamics. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** Discuss the **trade-offs** in choosing the adaptation gain. Explain how the **normalized adaptation law** overcomes the limitations of fixed-gain adaptation. **[(7 marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution:**

Plant: $\dot{y}_p = -a y_p + b u$ with $a = 2$, $b = 3$.
Model: $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$ with $a_m = 5$, $b_m = 6$.

Control law: $u = \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y_p$.

**Step 1 — Ideal parameters:**

$$\theta_1^* = \frac{b_m}{b} = \frac{6}{3} = 2, \qquad \theta_2^* = \frac{a_m - a}{b} = \frac{5 - 2}{3} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Error dynamics:**

$$\dot{e} = -5\,e + 3\,\phi_2 y_p - 3\,\phi_1 r$$

**Step 3 — Lyapunov function:**

$$V = e^2 + \frac{3}{\gamma_1}\phi_1^2 + \frac{3}{\gamma_2}\phi_2^2$$

**Step 4 — Adaptation laws:**

$$\dot{\theta}_1 = \gamma_1 e r, \qquad \dot{\theta}_2 = -\gamma_2 e y_p$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\dot{V}$:**

$$\dot{V} = -2a_m e^2 = -10 e^2 \le 0$$

Therefore, **any $\gamma_1 > 0$ and $\gamma_2 > 0$** guarantees asymptotic stability. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Solution:**

**Trade-offs in $\gamma$:**

| Small $\gamma$ | Large $\gamma$ |
|----------------|----------------|
| Slow convergence | Fast convergence |
| Robust to noise | Sensitive to noise |
| Smooth parameters | Oscillatory parameters |
| Sluggish response | Aggressive response |

**Limitations of fixed gain:**
- Cannot adapt to varying signal levels
- Causes **parameter drift** when regressor is small but bounded error exists
- Violation of persistent excitation leads to bursting

**Normalized adaptation law:**

$$\gamma_n = \frac{\gamma_0}{1 + \vert\vert\omega\vert\vert^2}$$

**Advantages:**
1. Effective gain $\gamma_n \cdot \vert\vert\omega\vert\vert^2 \le \gamma_0$ (bounded)
2. Robust to unmodeled dynamics
3. Automatic scaling with signal magnitude
4. Prevents parameter drift when regressor is large

This makes the system **practically robust** even when the ideal Lyapunov conditions are not met. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** (Alternative)

**(a)** Derive the **adaptation laws** for a first-order MRAC using the **Lyapunov stability method**, clearly showing how the adaptation gain enters the parameter update equations. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** Using the **Routh–Hurwitz criterion**, analyze the stability of a second-order adaptive system with adaptation gain $\gamma$ and determine the admissible range. **[(7 marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution:**

Refer to derivation in **Section 3.1, Steps 1–7** above.

Key results:

- Plant: $\dot{y}_p = -a y_p + b u$
- Model: $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$
- Error dynamics: $\dot{e} = -a_m e + b \phi^T \omega$
- Lyapunov: $V = e^2 + \dfrac{b}{\gamma}\phi^T\phi$
- Result: $\dot{V} = -2a_m e^2 \le 0 \quad \forall\, \gamma > 0$

Adaptation laws:

$$\dot{\theta}_1 = \gamma_1 e r, \qquad \dot{\theta}_2 = -\gamma_2 e y_p$$

The **adaptation gain** appears as a direct multiplier of the product $e \cdot \omega$, controlling the speed of parameter convergence. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Solution:**

Consider the second-order error dynamics with adaptation gain:

$$\ddot{e} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{e} + \omega_n^2 e = -\gamma\, e\,\omega^2$$

Closed-loop characteristic equation (after linearization):

$$s^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n + \gamma\omega_{max}^2)\,s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Routh array:**

| $s^2$ | 1 | $\omega_n^2$ |
|-------|---|--------------|
| $s^1$ | $2\zeta\omega_n + \gamma\omega_{max}^2$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $\omega_n^2$ | |

**Routh–Hurwitz conditions** (all first-column entries positive):

1. $1 > 0$ ✓ (always)
2. $2\zeta\omega_n + \gamma\,\omega_{max}^2 > 0$ ✓ (if $\zeta, \omega_n, \gamma > 0$)
3. $\omega_n^2 > 0$ ✓ (always)

**Admissible range:** $\boxed{\gamma > 0}$

However, practical bound:

$$\gamma < \gamma_{max} = \frac{2\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_{max}^2}$$

beyond which excessive overshoot and instability occur. **[7 marks]**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in adaptation law:** Students often write $\dot{\theta}_1 = -\gamma e r$ instead of $+\gamma e r$. The sign is **positive** because $\dot{\phi}_1$ must equal $+\gamma e r$ to cancel the cross-term in $\dot{V}$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\dfrac{b}{\gamma}$ factor in V:** The Lyapunov candidate must include the scaling $\dfrac{b}{\gamma_i}$ — writing simply $V = e^2 + \phi^T \phi$ will not yield clean cancellation.
>
> 3. **Claiming "any $\gamma > 0$ works always":** This is true **only in the ideal (noise-free, no unmodeled dynamics) case.** Examiners expect you to mention the **practical upper bound** for full marks.
>
> 4. **Not defining regressor $\omega$:** Always state $\omega = [r,\; y_p]^T$ explicitly before writing the adaptation law.
>
> 5. **Confusing $\theta$ and $\phi$:** $\theta$ is the controller parameter; $\phi = \theta - \theta^*$ is the parameter error. The adaptation law updates $\theta$, but $\dot{V}$ analysis uses $\phi$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Determination of Adaptation Gain**

- [x] **Definition:** $\gamma$ is the positive scalar/matrix scaling parameter updates in MRAC.
- [x] **Generic law:** $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = \gamma\,e\,\omega$ (gradient-based).
- [x] **MIT rule form:** $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma\,e\,\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$.
- [x] **Lyapunov function:** $V = e^2 + \dfrac{b}{\gamma}\phi^T\phi$ (positive-definite for $\gamma > 0$).
- [x] **Lyapunov derivative:** $\dot{V} = -2a_m e^2 \le 0$ ⇒ global asymptotic stability (ideal case).
- [x] **Ideal range:** $\gamma > 0$ (Lyapunov); practical upper bound $\gamma_{\max} = \dfrac{a_m}{b_{\max}\vert\vert\omega\vert\vert_{\max}^2}$.
- [x] **Adaptation laws for first-order plant:**
  - $\dot{\theta}_1 = +\gamma_1 e r$
  - $\dot{\theta}_2 = -\gamma_2 e y_p$
